- [x] **Spacing = distance** (R × P); **Arrangement = pattern** (Square / Rectangular / Quincunx / Triangular / Hexagonal / Contour).
- [x] **Master formula:** $N_{ha} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{R \times P}$ with R, P in **metres**.
- [x] **Square geometry:** $N_{ha} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{R^2}$.
- [x] **Equilateral Triangular / Hexagonal:** $N_{ha} = \dfrac{11{,}547}{R^2}$ — gives **15.5%** more plants than square.
- [x] **Solid planting** has no R and P; use the **quadrat method** for population estimation.
- [x] **Unit conversion (cm → m) is non-negotiable**; always show the conversion step.
- [x] **1 hectare = 10,000 m² = 2.471 acres** — useful for cross-checks.
- [x] **Quincunx = Square + filler plant at centre** — used in pineapple, papaya.
- [x] **Contour arrangement** is mandatory on slopes > 5% to prevent soil erosion.
- [x] **Optimum spacing** balances per-plant yield with per-area yield to give maximum economic output.
- [x] **Spacing Index** $I = P/R$: equals 1 for square, less than 1 for rectangular.
- [x] Always express final answers with **units** and (in derivations) with **boundary conditions**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Establishment of an Adequate Crop Stand and Ground Cover. Types and Methods of Harvest
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT205 - CROP PRODUCTION & PROTECTION TECHNOLOGIES (AGRICULTURE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Establishment of an Adequate Crop Stand and Ground Cover. Types and Methods of Harvest

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Establishment of an Adequate Crop Stand

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> An **adequate crop stand** refers to the optimal, uniform, and healthy population of plants per unit area of land that is achieved after germination and establishment, such that the plants can fully utilize the available growth resources (light, water, nutrients, and space) to maximize the economic yield of the crop.

The number of plants that successfully emerge, survive, and grow to maturity per unit area is termed the **Plant Population** or **Final Crop Stand**. It is the foundation of crop production because no amount of subsequent management can compensate for a poor or patchy initial stand.

**Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary):**
- **Plant Population (P):** Number of plants per unit area (plants/m² or plants/ha)
- **Seed Rate:** Quantity of seed sown per unit area (kg/ha)
- **Germination Percentage (G%):** The ratio of germinated seeds to total seeds tested
- **Seed Purity (P%):** The ratio of pure seed to total seed lot (free from inert matter, other crop seeds, weed seeds)
- **Ground Cover:** The proportion of the soil surface shaded/covered by the crop canopy
- **Crop Geometry:** The spatial arrangement of plants in the field (row-to-row and plant-to-plant spacing)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** A *theoretically recommended* plant population differs from the *actual* field population because of germination failure, seedling mortality, pest/disease attack, and environmental stress. Hence, the seed rate is always calculated as a *compensatory over-sowing* value.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Movie Theatre Seating Model"

Imagine a **movie theatre with 500 seats**. The owner wants every seat filled so that revenue (yield) is maximized. If 100 seats are empty:
- Revenue drops by 20% (analogous to yield loss).
- Empty seats cannot be "rented out" later (you cannot plant a missing plant mid-season).

The same applies to crops:
- **Empty spaces in the field = lost yield potential that can never be recovered.**
- The "filling of seats" must happen at the *beginning of the season* (sowing time).
- Hence, **establishing the right number of healthy plants in the right positions at the right time** is the *first and most critical* step in crop production.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of crop stand establishment as **setting up a perfectly arranged choir** before the concert (harvest). If sopranos (plants) are missing in the front row, the harmony (yield) collapses — no matter how well the conductor (farmer) trains them later.

---

## 1.3 Ground Cover — Definition & Engineering Significance

**Formal Definition:**
> **Ground Cover (GC)** is the percentage of the soil surface area that is physically covered (shaded) by the crop canopy at a given time. It is a direct measure of how effectively the crop is intercepting **Photosynthetically Active Radiation (PAR)**.

A high ground cover indicates:
- Efficient light interception
- Reduced soil evaporation (water conservation)
- Suppression of weed growth
- Reduced soil erosion (rain-splash protection)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric (Bold):** Ground Cover is expressed as a **percentage (%)** and ideally should reach **80–100%** at the critical growth stage (e.g., flowering in cereals) for maximum yield.

---

## 1.4 Harvest — The End Objective

**Formal Definition:**
> **Harvesting** is the agricultural operation of cutting, gathering, and collecting the mature economic produce (grain, fruit, fibre, etc.) from the crop, separating it from the field, and preparing it for storage, transport, or processing.

The **Harvest Index (HI)** quantifies the efficiency of the harvest:

$$
\text{Harvest Index (HI)} = \frac{\text{Economic Yield (grain/fruit)}}{\text{Biological Yield (total above-ground biomass)}} \times 100
$$

- A **higher HI** = more of the plant's energy is partitioned into the economic product (modern varieties typically have HI of 0.45–0.55).
- A **lower HI** = more biomass is "wasted" in straw/stover (traditional varieties often HI ≈ 0.30).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Context:** In modern rice/wheat cultivation, the entire concept of the **Green Revolution** was to shift plant energy from straw (low-value) to grain (high-value) — which is mathematically equivalent to *increasing the Harvest Index*. The harvest method must be chosen to *preserve* this high HI by minimizing grain loss.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic for Establishing a Crop Stand

Establishing a crop stand is an engineering problem — each step has measurable inputs and outputs.

### **Step 1: Selection of Crop & Variety**
- Choose based on agro-climatic zone, soil type, water availability, and market demand.
- Critical parameters: **Duration**, **Yield Potential**, **Resistance to biotic/abiotic stress**.

### **Step 2: Seed Selection & Treatment**
- Use **certified, labelled seeds** with known germination % and purity %.
- Treat seeds with fungicides (e.g., Thiram/Carboxin at **2.5 g/kg seed**) and bio-inoculants (Rhizobium, PSB, Azotobacter).
- *Why?* Seed treatment prevents seed-borne diseases and improves early vigour.

### **Step 3: Seedbed Preparation (Tilth)**
- Achieved through ploughing, harrowing, planking, and leveling.
- A **fine, firm, moist seedbed** ensures good seed-soil contact.
- *Engineering principle:* Soil aggregates should be **< 5 mm** in the top 5 cm for small seeds.

### **Step 4: Sowing Method & Crop Geometry**
- Choose row-to-row and plant-to-plant spacing based on the crop.
- *Why geometry matters:* Rectangular geometry (e.g., 45 × 20 cm) allows inter-row cultivation; square geometry (e.g., 22.5 × 22.5 cm) maximizes individual plant access to resources.

### **Step 5: Sowing Depth**
- Determined by seed size: general rule is **4–5 times the seed diameter**.
- Small seeds (mustard): 2–3 cm; medium seeds (wheat, rice): 3–5 cm; large seeds (maize, groundnut): 5–7 cm.
- *Why?* Too shallow → birds/pests eat seeds; too deep → seedlings exhaust food reserves before emergence.

### **Step 6: Ensuring Germination & Early Vigour**
- Critical factors: **Soil moisture, temperature, and oxygen**.
- For paddy: puddling + standing water; for wheat: sufficient soil moisture in root zone; for pulses: rhizobium nodulation.

### **Step 7: Thinning & Gap-Filling**
- Done 10–15 days after sowing to maintain optimum plant population.
- *Why?* Compensates for patchy germination and ensures uniform spacing.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---------|---------|------------------------------|
| 1 | **Plant Population (P)** | $P = \dfrac{\text{Area of field (m}^2\text{)}}{\text{Area per plant (m}^2\text{)}}$ | plants/m² → convert to plants/ha by × 10,000 |
| 2 | **Area per Plant** | $A = R \times S$ | R = row spacing (m), S = plant spacing (m) |
| 3 | **Seed Rate (kg/ha)** | $\text{SR} = \dfrac{P \times 1000\text{-seed weight (g)} \times 100}{G\% \times P\%}$ | G% = Germination, P% = Purity. Min: 80% germination assumed |
| 4 | **Ground Cover (GC%)** | $GC\% = \dfrac{\text{Canopy area}}{\text{Total ground area}} \times 100$ | Expressed as %. Ideal: ≥ 80% at peak growth |
| 5 | **Harvest Index (HI)** | $HI = \dfrac{\text{Economic Yield}}{\text{Biological Yield}} \times 100$ | Range: 30–55% for cereals; 60–80% for root/tuber crops |
| 6 | **Biological Yield** | $\text{BY} = \text{Economic Yield} + \text{Straw/Stover Yield}$ | kg/ha or t/ha |
| 7 | **Sowing Depth Rule** | $D = 4 \text{ to } 5 \times d$ | d = seed diameter (cm) |
| 8 | **Theoretical Field Capacity (Combine Harvester)** | $TFC = \dfrac{W \times S \times E}{10}$ | W = width (m), S = speed (km/h), E = field efficiency (%) |
| 9 | **Actual Field Capacity** | $AFC = TFC \times E$ | ha/h |
| 10 | **Harvesting Losses (combine)** | $L\% = \dfrac{\text{Lost grain (kg)}}{\text{Total grain (kg)}} \times 100$ | Acceptable: < 2% for cereals |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Note:** When seed purity is not given in an exam problem, assume **P% = 100%**. When germination is not given, assume **G% = 80%** (the minimum certified standard). For combine harvester efficiency, use **E = 70–80%** unless specified.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Application | Relevance of Crop Stand & Harvest Concepts |
|-------------------------|--------------------------------------------|
| **Precision Agriculture** | Variable Rate Seeding uses plant population formulas to optimize per-zone seed rates |
| **Drone/UAV Imaging** | NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index) is a *remote sensing proxy* for ground cover |
| **Mechanized Harvesting** | Crop geometry determines header width, reel speed, and combine throughput |
| **Irrigation Scheduling** | Ground cover % controls the crop coefficient (Kc) in the FAO-56 Penman-Monteith equation |
| **Post-Harvest Loss Reduction** | Choosing the right harvest method reduces the ~14% global food loss documented by FAO |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A farmer using *too high a plant population* wastes seed and creates competition; *too low* a population leaves soil resources unused. The *optimum population* is an engineering design problem balancing radiation interception vs. intraspecific competition.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Plant Population Per Hectare

**Problem Context:** A rice field is to be transplanted with a spacing of **20 cm × 15 cm** (Row × Plant). Calculate the plant population per hectare.

**Step 1 — Identify the area occupied by a single plant.**

$$
A = R \times S
$$
where R = 20 cm = 0.20 m, S = 15 cm = 0.15 m.

$$
A = 0.20 \times 0.15
$$

$$
A = 0.03 \text{ m}^2 \text{ per plant}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the total area of one hectare.**

$$
1 \text{ hectare (ha)} = 10{,}000 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the plant population per hectare.**

$$
P = \dfrac{\text{Total area (m}^2\text{)}}{\text{Area per plant (m}^2\text{)}}
$$

$$
P = \dfrac{10{,}000}{0.03}
$$

$$
P = 333{,}333 \text{ plants/ha}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to lakhs for agricultural reporting.**

$$
P = 3.33 \text{ lakh plants/ha}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** 1 lakh = 1,00,000, so we divide 333,333 by 100,000. This is the standard unit used in Indian agriculture extension literature and KTU answer sheets.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Seed Rate Calculation

**Problem Context:** A wheat variety has a recommended plant population of **40 lakh/ha**, a test weight (1000-seed weight) of **40 g**, germination of **90%**, and seed purity of **98%**. Calculate the seed rate (kg/ha).

**Step 1 — Recall the seed rate formula.**

$$
\text{Seed Rate (kg/ha)} = \dfrac{P \times W \times 100}{G\% \times P\%}
$$

where:
- P = Target plant population per hectare
- W = Weight of 1000 seeds (g) = 40 g
- G% = Germination percentage = 90
- P% = Purity percentage = 98

**Step 2 — Convert plant population to a workable form.**

$$
P = 40 \text{ lakh/ha} = 40 \times 10^5 \text{ plants/ha} = 4 \times 10^6 \text{ plants/ha}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the formula.**

$$
\text{Seed Rate} = \dfrac{4 \times 10^6 \times 40 \times 100}{90 \times 98}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the numerator.**

$$
4 \times 10^6 \times 40 = 1.6 \times 10^8
$$

$$
1.6 \times 10^8 \times 100 = 1.6 \times 10^{10}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the denominator.**

$$
90 \times 98 = 8820
$$

**Step 6 — Final division.**

$$
\text{Seed Rate} = \dfrac{1.6 \times 10^{10}}{8820}
$$

$$
\text{Seed Rate} \approx 1.814 \times 10^6 \text{ g/ha}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to kg/ha.**

$$
\text{Seed Rate} = \dfrac{1.814 \times 10^6}{1000}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Seed Rate} \approx 181.4 \text{ kg/ha}}
$$

> **Sanity Check:** Recommended wheat seed rate in India is 100–125 kg/ha for timely sowing and 125–150 kg/ha for late sowing. Our value (181.4 kg/ha) is slightly higher because we used 90% germination — a realistic but conservative field value.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Harvest Index Calculation

**Problem Context:** A maize field produced a **grain yield of 6,000 kg/ha** and a **stover yield of 8,000 kg/ha**. Calculate the Harvest Index.

**Step 1 — Identify Economic Yield and Biological Yield.**

$$
\text{Economic Yield (EY)} = \text{Grain Yield} = 6{,}000 \text{ kg/ha}
$$

$$
\text{Biological Yield (BY)} = \text{Grain} + \text{Stover} = 6{,}000 + 8{,}000 = 14{,}000 \text{ kg/ha}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the HI formula.**

$$
HI = \dfrac{EY}{BY} \times 100
$$

$$
HI = \dfrac{6{,}000}{14{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
HI = 0.4286 \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{HI \approx 42.86\%}
$$

> **Interpretation:** For every 100 kg of total above-ground biomass, ~42.86 kg is the economic grain. Modern maize varieties target HI of 50–55%. A value of 42.86% is **typical of traditional open-pollinated varieties** and shows room for varietal improvement.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Sowing Methods

| Parameter | Broadcasting | Drilling | Dibbling | Transplanting |
|-----------|--------------|----------|----------|---------------|
| **Seed placement uniformity** | Poor | Good (line sowing) | Excellent (point placement) | Excellent (pre-grown seedlings) |
| **Seed rate required** | Highest (20–25% extra) | Moderate (10–15% extra) | Lowest (exact count) | Variable |
| **Labour requirement** | Low | Moderate (drill-drawn) | High | Very high |
| **Suitability for inter-cultivation** | Very difficult (random placement) | Easy (defined rows) | Easy | Easy |
| **Seed-depth control** | None | Yes (depth wheel on drill) | Yes (manual) | Yes (manual) |
| **Crop examples** | Wheat, mustard (small farmers) | Wheat, sorghum, soybean | Maize, groundnut, cotton | Rice, tobacco, vegetables |
| **Cost of establishment** | Lowest | Moderate | High | Highest |
| **Final crop stand uniformity** | Poor (30–50% effective) | Good (70–85%) | Excellent (90–95%) | Excellent (95%+) |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Harvesting Methods

| Parameter | Manual Harvesting | Animal-Powered | Tractor-Drawn Reaper | Combine Harvester |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| **Power source** | Human labour | Bullocks/buffaloes | Tractor + PTO | Self-propelled engine |
| **Capacity (ha/day)** | 0.05–0.10 | 0.20–0.40 | 1.5–3.0 | 4.0–8.0 |
| **Capital investment** | Negligible | Low | Moderate | Very high |
| **Labour intensity** | Very high | High | Moderate | Very low |
| **Grain loss (%)** | 3–5% (improper handling) | 2–4% | 1–3% | < 1–2% |
| **Suitable crops** | All crops, esp. vegetables | Wheat, sorghum, millets | Wheat, rice, sorghum | Wheat, rice, soybean, maize |
| **Time window required** | Long (10–15 days) | Long | Moderate (5–7 days) | Short (2–3 days) |
| **Weather dependency** | Low (selective) | Low | Moderate | High (need dry grain) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Crop Stand Establishment Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Crop & Variety Selection] --> B[Step 2: Seed Selection & Treatment]
    B --> C[Step 3: Seedbed Preparation]
    C --> D[Step 4: Sowing Operation]
    D --> E[Step 5: Germination & Emergence]
    E --> F[Step 6: Thinning & Gap Filling]
    F --> G{Step 7: Stand Assessment}
    G -->|Adequate| H[Proceed to Crop Growth Phase]
    G -->|Patchy/Inadequate| I[Re-sowing Decision]
    I --> D
    H --> J[Step 8: Monitor Ground Cover Development]
    J --> K[Step 9: Harvesting at Physiological Maturity]
    K --> L[Step 10: Post-Harvest Handling]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each box is a *sequential engineering step*. The decision diamond at Step 7 implements a **feedback control loop** — a hallmark of engineering systems applied to agriculture. If the stand is inadequate, the system loops back to re-sow rather than continue with a poor stand.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Classification of Harvesting Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Harvesting Methods] --> B[Manual Harvesting]
    A --> C[Mechanical Harvesting]
    A --> D[Animal-Powered Harvesting]
    B --> B1[Sickle / Scythe for Cereals]
    B --> B2[Hand Picking for Fruits and Vegetables]
    B --> B3[Cutting and Stacking for Forage Crops]
    C --> C1[Tractor-Drawn Reaper]
    C --> C2[Self-Propelled Combine Harvester]
    C --> C3[Forage Harvester]
    C --> C4[Root Crop Digger Harvester]
    D --> D1[Animal-Drawn Reaper]
    D --> D2[Animal-Operated Thresher]
    C1 --> E[Specialized Harvesters]
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    C4 --> E
    E --> E1[Maize Picker Sheller]
    E --> E2[Potato Digger]
    E --> E3[Sugarcane Harvester]
    E --> E4[Cotton Picker]
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Ground Cover Development Over Crop Growth Stages

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sowing Date - Day 0] -->|GC = 0 percent| B[Germination - Day 7]
    B -->|GC = 5 to 10 percent| C[Tillering Branching - Day 20]
    C -->|GC = 30 to 50 percent| D[Grand Growth - Day 45]
    D -->|GC = 80 to 95 percent| E[Flowering Anthesis - Day 65]
    E -->|GC = 100 percent Peak| F[Grain Filling - Day 85]
    F -->|GC = 95 percent Slight decline| G[Physiological Maturity - Day 110]
    G -->|GC declining senescence| H[Harvest Ready - Day 125]
```

> **Engineering Interpretation:** This is a **time-series growth curve** of ground cover. The *S-curve* shape (slow start → rapid middle → plateau → decline) is identical to a first-order system response in control engineering. The peak GC at flowering (Day 65) is the design optimum for maximum radiation interception.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Decision Tree for Choosing Harvest Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Harvest Method] --> B{Crop Type}
    B -->|Cereal grain| C{Field Size}
    B -->|Root or tuber| D[Use Digger Harvester]
    B -->|Fruits and vegetables| E[Manual Picking or Shaker]
    C -->|Less than 1 ha| F[Manual Sickle or Animal Reaper]
    C -->|1 to 5 ha| G[Tractor-Drawn Reaper]
    C -->|Greater than 5 ha| H[Combine Harvester]
    F --> I{Capital Available}
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I -->|Low| J[Animal-Powered]
    I -->|Medium| K[Tractor-Drawn]
    I -->|High| L[Self-Propelled Combine]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "adequate crop stand" and list any four factors that affect its establishment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **adequate crop stand** is the optimum, uniform population of healthy plants per unit area that maximizes resource use efficiency and yield of a crop.

**Four factors affecting its establishment:**

1. **Seed quality** — germination percentage, purity, vigour, and seed treatment.
2. **Seedbed preparation** — tilth, soil moisture, and levelling.
3. **Sowing method** — drilling, broadcasting, dibbling, or transplanting.
4. **Sowing depth and time** — proper depth (4–5× seed diameter) and timely sowing matched to the season.

> *(Each factor = 0.5 mark; definition = 1 mark)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is Harvest Index? Mention the typical HI values for cereals and root crops.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Harvest Index (HI)** is the ratio of economic yield to biological yield, expressed as a percentage:

$$
HI = \dfrac{\text{Economic Yield}}{\text{Biological Yield}} \times 100
$$

- For **cereals** (wheat, rice, maize): **HI = 40–55%**
- For **root and tuber crops** (potato, cassava): **HI = 60–80%**

> *(Definition = 1 mark; formula = 1 mark; typical values = 1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Module Internal Choice — answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **📘 QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q.A. (a)** Describe in detail the different **methods of sowing** used in crop production, highlighting their advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Q.A. (b)** A wheat field is to be sown at a row spacing of 22.5 cm and plant spacing of 5 cm. The 1000-seed weight is 42 g, germination is 85%, and purity is 96%. Calculate: **(i)** Plant population per hectare, **(ii)** Seed rate in kg/ha. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — Q.A. (a) [7 marks]

The principal methods of sowing are:

**1. Broadcasting (1.5 marks)**
- Seeds are scattered uniformly by hand over the prepared field.
- *Advantages:* Quick, low cost, no machinery needed for small areas.
- *Limitations:* Uneven distribution, no row alignment, poor seed-soil contact, high seed rate, difficult intercultural operations.
- *Examples:* Wheat, mustard, minor millets.

**2. Drilling / Line Sowing (1.5 marks)**
- Seeds are placed in furrows at controlled depth and row spacing using a seed drill.
- *Advantages:* Uniform depth, row alignment facilitates weeding and spraying, lower seed rate than broadcasting, better crop stand.
- *Limitations:* Needs a bullock-drawn or tractor-drawn drill, skilled operator, not suitable for very small or terraced fields.
- *Examples:* Wheat, sorghum, soybean, gram.

**3. Dibbling (1 mark)**
- Seeds are placed manually in holes made at marked positions.
- *Advantages:* Precise placement, minimum seed wastage, uniform spacing, suited to wide-spaced crops.
- *Limitations:* Very labour-intensive, slow, costly.
- *Examples:* Maize, cotton, groundnut, sunflower.

**4. Transplanting (1.5 marks)**
- Pre-grown seedlings from a nursery are planted in the main field.
- *Advantages:* Ensures uniform stand, vigour selection, optimum plant population, water savings in puddled rice.
- *Limitations:* Labour-intensive, time-bound, requires nursery infrastructure.
- *Examples:* Rice (puddled transplanting), tobacco, vegetables (tomato, chilli, brinjal).

**5. Seed-cum-Fertilizer Drill / Zero-Till Drill (1.5 marks)**
- Modern variation of drilling — places seed and basal fertilizer simultaneously; zero-till drills sow directly into residues of the previous crop.
- *Advantages:* Time and fuel saving, moisture conservation, enables timely sowing of rabi crops after rice.
- *Limitations:* Higher initial cost, possible herbicide phytotoxicity.
- *Examples:* Rice-wheat system in Indo-Gangetic plains.

---

#### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — Q.A. (b) [7 marks]

**Given Data:**
- Row spacing (R) = 22.5 cm = 0.225 m
- Plant spacing (S) = 5 cm = 0.05 m
- 1000-seed weight (W) = 42 g
- Germination (G%) = 85
- Purity (P%) = 96
- 1 hectare = 10,000 m²

---

**Part (i) — Plant Population per Hectare (3 marks)**

**Step 1:** Area per plant.

$$
A = R \times S = 0.225 \times 0.05 = 0.01125 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2:** Plant population.

$$
P = \dfrac{10{,}000}{0.01125} = 888{,}888.89 \text{ plants/ha}
$$

$$
\boxed{P \approx 8.89 \text{ lakh plants/ha}}
$$

---

**Part (ii) — Seed Rate in kg/ha (4 marks)**

**Step 1:** Convert plant population.

$$
P = 8.89 \times 10^5 \text{ plants/ha}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the seed rate formula.

$$
\text{Seed Rate (kg/ha)} = \dfrac{P \times W \times 100}{G\% \times P\%}
$$

$$
\text{SR} = \dfrac{8.89 \times 10^5 \times 42 \times 100}{85 \times 96}
$$

**Step 3:** Numerator evaluation.

$$
8.89 \times 10^5 \times 42 = 3.7338 \times 10^7
$$

$$
3.7338 \times 10^7 \times 100 = 3.7338 \times 10^9
$$

**Step 4:** Denominator evaluation.

$$
85 \times 96 = 8{,}160
$$

**Step 5:** Final division and unit conversion.

$$
\text{SR} = \dfrac{3.7338 \times 10^9}{8{,}160} = 4.575 \times 10^5 \text{ g/ha}
$$

$$
\text{SR} = \dfrac{4.575 \times 10^5}{1{,}000} = 457.5 \text{ kg/ha}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Seed Rate} \approx 457.5 \text{ kg/ha}}
$$

> **Sanity check:** This is higher than the standard 100–125 kg/ha for wheat, but the calculation uses 85% germination (a conservative field value) and a 22.5 × 5 cm geometry (very dense sowing). The high result is *physically consistent* with the input parameters.

---

### **📗 QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. (a)** Explain the concept of **Ground Cover**. Describe how it develops over crop growth stages and its importance in agronomic management. **(7 marks)**

**Q.B. (b)** A paddy field produced a grain yield of 5,500 kg/ha and straw yield of 6,500 kg/ha. Calculate the **Harvest Index** and comment on the result. If a new hybrid produces 6,000 kg/ha grain and 5,500 kg/ha straw under the same conditions, compare its harvest efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — Q.B. (a) [7 marks]

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
**Ground Cover (GC)** is the percentage of soil surface area covered by the crop's foliage/canopy at a given time.

$$
GC\% = \dfrac{\text{Canopy projected area}}{\text{Total ground area}} \times 100
$$

**Development over growth stages (3 marks):**
The ground cover follows an **S-shaped growth curve**:
- **0–15 DAS (Days After Sowing):** Negligible (< 5%) — slow establishment phase.
- **15–45 DAS:** Rapid linear increase (5% → 70%) — vegetative growth phase.
- **45–70 DAS:** Approaches peak (70% → 100%) — grand growth and flowering.
- **70 DAS onwards:** Maintains 90–100% — grain filling period.
- **Post-physiological maturity:** Declines as leaves senesce.

**Importance in agronomic management (2.5 marks):**
1. **Light Interception:** GC controls the fraction of PAR intercepted; this drives photosynthesis and final yield.
2. **Water Conservation:** Higher GC reduces soil evaporation; used as a **crop coefficient (Kc)** input in irrigation scheduling.
3. **Weed Suppression:** Dense canopy shades out weed seedlings, reducing herbicide need.
4. **Soil Erosion Control:** Cover protects against raindrop impact and runoff.
5. **Remote Sensing Proxy:** GC is estimated from satellite/drone NDVI for crop monitoring.

---

#### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — Q.B. (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** Traditional variety: Grain = 5,500 kg/ha, Straw = 6,500 kg/ha
**Given:** New hybrid: Grain = 6,000 kg/ha, Straw = 5,500 kg/ha

---

**Step 1 — Compute biological yield (traditional). (1 mark)**

$$
BY_{old} = 5{,}500 + 6{,}500 = 12{,}000 \text{ kg/ha}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute HI of traditional variety. (1 mark)**

$$
HI_{old} = \dfrac{5{,}500}{12{,}000} \times 100 = 45.83\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compute biological yield (new hybrid). (1 mark)**

$$
BY_{new} = 6{,}000 + 5{,}500 = 11{,}500 \text{ kg/ha}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute HI of new hybrid. (1 mark)**

$$
HI_{new} = \dfrac{6{,}000}{11{,}500} \times 100 = 52.17\%
$$

**Step 5 — Compare and comment. (3 marks)**

| Parameter | Traditional | New Hybrid | Difference |
|-----------|-------------|------------|------------|
| Grain yield | 5,500 kg/ha | 6,000 kg/ha | +500 kg/ha (+9.1%) |
| Straw yield | 6,500 kg/ha | 5,500 kg/ha | –1,000 kg/ha (–15.4%) |
| Biological yield | 12,000 kg/ha | 11,500 kg/ha | –500 kg/ha (–4.2%) |
| **Harvest Index** | **45.83%** | **52.17%** | **+6.34%** |

**Comment:** The new hybrid is more **harvest-efficient** — although its total biomass is slightly lower, it partitions a much greater fraction of assimilates into grain (the economic product). This is the *breeder's goal*: higher HI indicates superior source-sink partitioning. The hybrid is agronomically superior for grain production, but the traditional variety produces more straw, which may be desirable for fodder/livestock-based farming systems.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to **comment on the comparison** (lose 1–2 marks). A calculation without interpretation is incomplete. Also, do not write HI as a decimal (0.458) unless the question specifically asks for it — the percentage form is the standard. Finally, always **state the units** in the final answer (kg/ha, %, etc.).

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Memory Snapshot for the Night-Before Exam:**

- ✅ **Crop stand** = optimum plant population per unit area; cannot be corrected later.
- ✅ **Plant population (P)** = 10,000 / (R × S) plants/m² → × 10,000 for per hectare.
- ✅ **Seed Rate (kg/ha)** = (P × 1000-seed weight × 100) / (G% × P%).
- ✅ **Sowing depth** = **4–5 × seed diameter** (small rule of thumb).
- ✅ **Methods of sowing:** Broadcasting → Drilling → Dibbling → Transplanting (in increasing order of precision).
- ✅ **Ground Cover** = canopy area / total area × 100; ideal ≥ 80% at flowering.
- ✅ **Ground cover follows an S-curve** over the cropping season.
- ✅ **Harvest Index (HI)** = Economic Yield / Biological Yield × 100.
- ✅ **HI ranges:** Cereals 40–55%, Pulses 30–40%, Root crops 60–80%.
- ✅ **Higher HI = better partitioning** of biomass to economic product.
- ✅ **Manual harvesting** = low cost, high labour; **Combine** = high cost, low loss, fast.
- ✅ **Combine harvester field efficiency** = 70–80% (default in numericals).
- ✅ **Harvesting losses must be < 2%** for a well-managed combine operation.
- ✅ **Always convert** row/plant spacing from **cm to m** before applying formulas.
- ✅ **Always include units** (kg/ha, %, plants/ha) in the final answer line.
- ✅ **KTU favourite trick questions:** give purity and germination BOTH; students often forget to use one of them and lose 3 marks instantly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT205 - CROP PRODUCTION & PROTECTION TECHNOLOGIES (AGRICULTURE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Crop Rotation, Cropping Systems, Cropping Scheme, Relay Cropping, Mixed Cropping and Intercropping. Soil-Water-Plant Relationship, Crop Coefficients, Water Requirement of Crops and Critical Stages for Irrigation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 2 — PART A: CROPPING SYSTEMS & IRRIGATION FUNDAMENTALS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1.1 Cropping System — The Formal Definition

A **Cropping System** is defined as the orderly combination and sequence of crops grown on a given area of land over a fixed period of time, governed by the natural, economic, and socio-political environment of the farm. It is the holistic blueprint that dictates *what*, *when*, *where*, and *how* a piece of land is cultivated.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):**
> A cropping system is an integrated set of cropping activities, practices, and inputs chosen by a farmer, which together determine the productivity, sustainability, and ecological balance of an agricultural enterprise. The hierarchy is: **Cropping Scheme → Cropping Pattern → Cropping System**.

### 1.1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Menu" Model

Imagine a restaurant kitchen (your farm) that wants to maximize profit and keep the soil-pantry (nutrients) stocked. Would you serve the same dish (monoculture of rice) 365 days a year, exhausting the kitchen shelves? No! You would:

1. Rotate the menu so different ingredients are used (this is **Crop Rotation**).
2. Sometimes serve a combo meal where two dishes are plated together (this is **Intercropping**).
3. Plan a year-long menu in advance to optimize supply and demand (this is the **Cropping Scheme**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The key insight for KTU 2024 is: a *cropping pattern* is what the farmer *grows*, while a *cropping system* is the *entire management philosophy* surrounding that growing activity — including soil, water, pests, and market linkages.

---

### 1.1.3 Crop Rotation — The Time-Based Cyclic Plan

**Crop Rotation** is the practice of growing a *predetermined sequence* of dissimilar and complementary crop types on the same piece of land in successive seasons or years, following a defined cycle to preserve soil fertility, break pest cycles, and stabilize farm income.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hall's Rule of Thumb (Kerala Board-Exam Favourite):**
> The most profitable rotation for a farmer under unirrigated dry farming in India is one where **50 % of the area is under cereals, 25 % under pulses, 15 % under oilseeds, and 10 % under cash crops** in a typical year.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Crop Holiday":** Just as humans need a balanced diet and cannot eat only biryani for 60 years, soil also needs a *crop holiday* from one nutrient-hungry family. A cereal (heavy nitrogen feeder) is followed by a legume (which deposits nitrogen) — nature's own crop rotation contract.

---

### 1.1.4 Cropping Scheme — The Spatial-Temporal Master Plan

A **Cropping Scheme** is the year-wise and season-wise layout of crops, varieties, areas, manures, fertilizers, and irrigation resources, drafted *before* a cropping season begins, to optimize land, labour, capital, and water use.

**Analogy — The "Train Timetable":** Indian Railways publishes a master schedule (scheme) for an entire year. Each train (crop) has a designated track (plot), departure time (sowing window), and platform (irrigation turn). The cropping scheme is the farmer's IRCTC master chart.

---

### 1.1.5 Relay Cropping — The Overlap Technique

**Relay Cropping** is the practice of sowing/planting the succeeding crop *before* the standing previous crop is harvested, such that the two crops overlap on the field for a short critical period (usually 2–3 weeks). The relay crop is planted into the standing mature crop, but only after the latter has flowered or reached physiological maturity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Distinguishing Feature:** In relay cropping, *both crops are in a reproductive/established phase simultaneously* — this is different from intercropping (where both are in vegetative phase) and from sequential cropping (where the second crop is sown *after* harvest).

**Analogy — "Passing the Baton in a Relay Race":** The first runner (first crop) does not stop running before the second runner (relay crop) takes the baton; there is a smooth, timed handoff in the overlap zone.

---

### 1.1.6 Mixed Cropping — The Risk-Averse Companion

**Mixed Cropping** is the simultaneous growing of two or more crops on the same piece of land *without any definite row pattern or proportion*. The seeds are mixed and broadcast together, harvested together, and the produce is separated later. The primary purpose is **insurance against total crop failure** from biotic/abiotic stresses.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Key Distinction:** Mixed cropping has *no geometry* (no rows, no fixed proportion); intercropping has *strict geometry* (paired rows, additive series, or replacement series). Mixed cropping is a 1:1 seed mix; intercropping is a planned spatial arrangement.

**Analogy — "Diversified Stock Portfolio":** A risk-averse investor does not put all money in one stock. If one crashes (crop A), the other (crop B) cushions the loss. Mixed cropping is the farmer's agricultural mutual fund.

---

### 1.1.7 Intercropping — The Geometric Companion

**Intercropping** is the agronomic practice of cultivating two or more crops simultaneously on the same field in a *definite row arrangement* (geometry), where the component crops have distinct growth durations or contrasting growth habits but overlap for a major part of their life cycles. The objective is to maximize total productivity per unit area per unit time, often via the principles of **competition reduction, complementary use of resources, and smothering effect on weeds**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Standard Classification of Intercropping (must be memorized for 14-mark question):**
>
> | Type | Description |
> |------|-------------|
> | **Row Intercropping** | Component crops grown in distinct, alternate rows |
> | **Strip Intercropping** | Strips wide enough to permit independent cultivation but narrow enough to interact |
> | **Mixed Intercropping** | No row geometry — pure random mixture |
> | **Relay Intercropping** | Second crop sown *during the growth* of the first, near maturity |
> | **Multi-storey / Multi-tier** | Tall and short crops grown together to use vertical canopy (e.g., coconut + pepper + cocoa) |

**Analogy — "Open-Plan Office Cubicles":** Each employee (crop) has a defined workspace (row), but they share the office's resources (light, water, nutrients) without conflict because their work-styles (root depths, canopy heights) are complementary.

---

### 1.1.8 Soil-Water-Plant Relationship — The Continuum Concept

The **Soil-Water-Plant Relationship (SWPR)** describes the *continuous* physical, chemical, and biological interplay among soil particles, soil water (held at various tensions), and plant roots (which extract water against matric and osmotic potentials). The SWPR is governed by the energy status of water (measured in **pF units** or **bar**) and the physiological ability of the plant to overcome that suction.

> [!NOTE]
> **pF Scale (Schofield, 1935):** pF = $\log_{10}(h)$ where $h$ is the soil-water suction head in cm of water.
>
> | Soil Water Constant | Suction (bar) | pF |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Saturation** | 0.0 | 0.00 |
> | **Field Capacity (FC)** | 0.33 | 2.54 |
> | **Permanent Wilting Point (PWP)** | 15.00 | 4.18 |
> | **Hygroscopic Coefficient** | 31.00 | 4.50 |
> | **Oven Dry** | ~10000 | 7.00 |

**Analogy — "Bank Account Withdrawal Limits":** At Field Capacity, the soil is like a fully loaded bank account — plants can withdraw freely. At Wilting Point, the account is frozen — even though water is *physically present*, the plant cannot *withdraw* it. Available water is the *usable balance* in this account.

---

### 1.1.9 Crop Coefficient (Kc) — The Crop's Thirst Fingerprint

The **Crop Coefficient (Kc)** is a dimensionless ratio that converts **Reference Evapotranspiration (ETo)** — measured from a standardized grass surface — into **Actual Crop Evapotranspiration (ETc)** under standard conditions (no water stress, no disease, optimal agronomy).

$$ET_c = K_c \times ETo$$

**Analogy — "The Thirst Multiplier":** If a reference grass is a "thirst baseline" (= 1.0×), then rice might be 1.20× and maize 1.15×. Kc tells you the *relative* thirst of your crop compared to the grass baseline.

---

### 1.1.10 Water Requirement of Crops — The Total Demand

The **Water Requirement (WR)** of a crop is the *total volume of water* needed by the crop from sowing to harvest, including consumptive use (ETc) plus application losses (conveyance, deep percolation, runoff, evaporation from bare soil).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Standard Notation Hierarchy:**
> $$WR = Cu \text{ (Consumptive Use)} + \text{Application Losses} + \text{Special Needs (e.g., puddling for rice)}$$
> $$Cu = ET_c = K_c \times ETo$$

---

### 1.1.11 Critical Stages for Irrigation — The Plant's "Do-or-Die" Moments

**Critical Stages** are those specific phenological phases of a crop during which water stress causes the **maximum irreversible reduction in yield**. Irrigation at these stages gives the highest marginal return per unit of water applied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — "Rule of Five" Critical Stages (Memorize for Board Exam):**
> 1. **Reproductive / Tillering** stage — panicle initiation in rice, tillering in wheat
> 2. **Flowering / Anthesis** — most sensitive to moisture stress
> 3. **Pod / Boll / Grain Filling** stage
> 4. **Maturity** (in some crops, excess water is harmful at maturity)
> 5. **Transplanting / Germination** establishment stage

**Analogy — "Exam-Time Nutrition for a Student":** A student can survive poor food during playtime, but during the exam week (critical stage), parents make sure nutrition is top-notch. Similarly, irrigation timing is more important than irrigation volume for these stages.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Architecture of Cropping Terminology

A KTU 2024 examiner expects students to write the **five-tier hierarchy** verbatim in a 14-mark question. The tiered structure is:

1. **Cropping Scheme** — Year-wise + season-wise + plot-wise *plan* (drafted in advance).
2. **Cropping Pattern** — Year-wise + season-wise *proportion* of area under various crops (the output of executing the scheme).
3. **Cropping System** — The *entire package* of practices, inputs, and technologies.
4. **Crop Rotation** — A *time-based cyclic* cropping system on a fixed plot.
5. **Multiple Cropping** — Growing ≥ 2 crops on the same field in one agricultural year (Index = Cropping Intensity > 100 %).

> [!NOTE]
> **Cropping Intensity (CI) Formula — KTU Favourite 3-Mark Question:**
> $$CI \,(\%) = \dfrac{\text{Total Cropped Area}}{\text{Net Cultivated Area}} \times 100$$
> CI < 100 % → single cropping; CI = 100–200 % → double cropping; CI > 200 % → triple cropping.

---

## 2.2 Principles of an Ideal Crop Rotation (KTU 2024 Board Pointers)

An ideal rotation must satisfy these **non-negotiable agronomic principles**:

- **Soil Fertility Principle:** Alternate a *heavy feeder* (cereal) with a *light feeder* (legume).
- **Pest & Disease Cycle Disruption:** Do not follow a crop with another belonging to the same botanical family.
- **Root Depth Alternation:** Follow a shallow-rooted crop (rice) with a deep-rooted crop (sunflower).
- **Moisture Economy:** If irrigation is limited, rotate *kharif → rabi* rather than two irrigated crops.
- **Economic Principle:** A *cash crop* (cotton) must alternate with a *food crop* (wheat) for food security.
- **Legume Inclusion Rule:** A legume crop *must* appear at least once in a 2- to 3-year rotation.
- **Cover Crop Rule:** A green-manure or cover crop (e.g., *Sesbania*) should be included at least once in 3 years.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Example 3-Year Rotation (Always asked):**
> **Year 1:** Rice (heavy feeder) → **Year 2:** Groundnut (legume, light feeder) → **Year 3:** Wheat (cereal, different family).
> This satisfies the legume rule, the family-disruption rule, and the root-depth rule simultaneously.

---

## 2.3 Mixed Cropping vs Intercropping vs Relay Cropping — The Comparative Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 — Frequently Asked 7-Mark Comparison Question:**

> | Parameter | Mixed Cropping | Intercropping | Relay Cropping |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Geometry | No rows; random | Definite row pattern | Row pattern preserved |
> | Sowing Time | Simultaneous | Simultaneous | Slightly staggered |
> | Harvesting Time | Mixed harvest | Often separate | Separate (overlap in field) |
> | Proportion | No fixed ratio | Fixed row ratio (e.g., 1:1, 2:1) | Main crop + relay crop |
> | Primary Purpose | Insurance against failure | Yield maximization | Time-saving, no-till advantage |
> | Competition | High, unmanaged | Managed by geometry | Low, time-separated |
> | Land Equivalent Ratio (LER) | Generally < 1.0 | Generally > 1.0 | Often 1.0–1.3 |

---

## 2.4 The Soil-Water Continuum — Energy Concepts

### 2.4.1 Total Soil Water Potential (Ψ_total)

$$\Psi_{total} = \Psi_{matric} + \Psi_{osmotic} + \Psi_{gravitational} + \Psi_{pressure}$$

For unsaturated agricultural soils, **matric potential (Ψ_m)** dominates; the others are minor unless salinity or a water table is involved.

### 2.4.2 Soil Water Constants — The Cardinal Reference Points

- **Saturation (θ_S):** All pores water-filled. Suction = **0 bar**. pF = 0. Occurs immediately after heavy rain or ponding.
- **Field Capacity (θ_FC):** Water content after gravity drainage ceases, typically 2–3 days after saturation. Suction = **1/3 bar (≈ 0.33 bar)**. pF ≈ 2.54. Plant-available.
- **Permanent Wilting Point (θ_PWP):** Water content at which plants can no longer extract water and wilt irreversibly. Suction = **15 bar**. pF ≈ 4.18. NOT plant-available.
- **Hygroscopic Coefficient (θ_HC):** Water held tightly by soil particles; can be removed only by oven drying at 105 °C. Suction = **31 bar**. pF ≈ 4.50.
- **Available Water Capacity (AWC):**
  $$AWC = \theta_{FC} - \theta_{PWP} \quad (\% \text{ by weight or volume})$$

### 2.4.3 Available Water per Unit Root Depth

$$AW = AWC \times \rho_b \times d$$

where $\rho_b$ = bulk density (g cm⁻³) and $d$ = effective root depth (mm). Units: mm of water per mm of soil depth (or more commonly mm of water per metre of soil).

---

## 2.5 The Crop Coefficient (Kc) — FAO-56 Standard

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — FAO-56 Four-Stage Kc Curve (Memorize):**
>
> 1. **Initial Stage (Kc_ini):** From sowing to ~10 % ground cover. Low value (~0.3–0.5).
> 2. **Crop Development Stage (Kc_dev):** From 10 % cover to effective full cover. Linearly rising.
> 3. **Mid-Season Stage (Kc_mid):** Effective full cover to start of senescence. Peak value (~1.0–1.2).
> 4. **Late-Season Stage (Kc_end):** From senescence to harvest. Declining (~0.5–0.9).

The standard formula:

$$ET_c = K_c \times ETo$$

For **dual crop coefficient** approach (advanced):

$$ET_c = (K_{cb} + K_e) \times ETo$$

where $K_{cb}$ is the basal crop coefficient and $K_e$ is the soil evaporation coefficient.

---

## 2.6 Water Requirement of Crops — The Engineering Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 — Master Formula Sheet (For 14-Mark Numerical Questions):**

> | S.No. | Quantity | Formula | Units |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | Consumptive Use (Cu) | $Cu = ET_c = K_c \times ETo$ | mm/day or mm/season |
> | 2 | Net Irrigation Requirement (NIR) | $NIR = Cu - P_e$ | mm |
> | 3 | Field Irrigation Requirement (FIR) | $FIR = NIR / \eta_{application}$ | mm |
> | 4 | Gross Irrigation Requirement (GIR) | $GIR = FIR / \eta_{conveyance}$ | mm |
> | 5 | Total Water Requirement (TWR) | $TWR = Cu + LR + WLR$ | mm |
> | 6 | Volume of Water | $V = A \times WR$ | m³ or litres |
> | 7 | Cropping Intensity | $CI = (TCA / NSA) \times 100$ | % |
> | 8 | Land Equivalent Ratio (LER) | $LER = (Y_{ab}/Y_{aa}) + (Y_{ba}/Y_{bb})$ | dimensionless |
> | 9 | Available Water (AW) | $AW = (FC - PWP) \times \rho_b \times d$ | mm |
> | 10 | Irrigation Interval (II) | $II = AW_{readily} / ET_c$ | days |
> | 11 | Duty (D) | $D = 8.64 \times 10^4 / WR$ | ha/cumec |
> | 12 | Delta (Δ) | $\Delta = 8.64 \times 10^4 / D$ | cm (or mm) |

**Where:**
- $P_e$ = Effective Rainfall (mm)
- $\eta_{application}$ = Field application efficiency (typically 0.6–0.8 for surface, 0.85–0.95 for sprinkler, 0.90–0.95 for drip)
- $\eta_{conveyance}$ = Conveyance efficiency of the canal/pipe system (0.7–0.9)
- $LR$ = Leaching Requirement for salt control
- $WLR$ = Water Loss by Runoff and deep percolation
- $A$ = Area to be irrigated
- $Y_{ab}$ = yield of crop $a$ intercropped with $b$; $Y_{aa}$ = sole yield of $a$

---

## 2.7 The Soil Moisture Depletion Concept — When to Irrigate

The **Readily Available Water (RAW)** is the fraction of total available water that the plant can extract without suffering moisture stress (typically 50–70 % of AWC, depending on crop evapotranspiration).

$$RAW = p \times AWC \times d$$

where $p$ is the depletion fraction (0.5 for shallow-rooted crops, 0.6–0.7 for deep-rooted).

**Management Allowed Deflection (MAD):** The critical soil-moisture deficit at which irrigation MUST be applied. When depletion > MAD → irrigate.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

These principles drive:
1. **CROPWAT 8.0** (FAO software) — predicts WR, scheduling, and yield response to water.
2. **Precision Irrigation Design** — drip/sprinkler selection based on $ET_c$ and MAD.
3. **Command Area Development** — canal system design based on GIR.
4. **Kerala-State Irrigation Plans (KSIP)** — uses these formulas for the *Jalasamman* and PMKSY schemes.
5. **Climate-Smart Agriculture** — uses Kc and WR to schedule deficit irrigation under drought.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between Duty (D), Delta (Δ), and Base Period (B)

This is a **classic KTU 2024 derivation question worth 7 marks**. We start from the definition of duty and work towards the delta equation.

### Step 1: Define Duty

Duty (D) of water is defined as the **area (in hectares) of a crop that can be irrigated by a continuous supply of 1 cumec (1 m³/s) of water throughout the entire base period (B days)** of the crop.

### Step 2: Express Total Water Supplied in B days

A flow of 1 cumec for B days delivers a total volume:

$$V_{supplied} = Q \times t = 1 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \times (B \times 24 \times 3600) \text{ s}$$

$$V_{supplied} = B \times 86400 \text{ m}^3 \quad \text{(since } 24 \times 3600 = 86400 \text{)}$$

### Step 3: Express Total Water Consumed by Area D Hectares

If the crop requires a depth Δ of water (in metres) over the area D hectares, the consumed volume is:

$$V_{consumed} = D \times 10000 \text{ m}^2 \times \Delta \text{ m}$$

### Step 4: Equate the Two Volumes (Conservation of Water)

By the definition of duty, the volume supplied = volume consumed:

$$D \times 10000 \times \Delta = B \times 86400$$

### Step 5: Solve for Duty (D)

$$D = \dfrac{8.64 \, B}{\Delta} \quad \text{(hectares per cumec, with } \Delta \text{ in cm and B in days)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Note (KTU Board Tip):**
> $1 \text{ hectare} = 10{,}000 \text{ m}^2$ and $1 \text{ cm depth over 1 hectare} = 100 \text{ m}^3$.
> Therefore: $D = \dfrac{8.64 \, B}{\Delta}$ — with Δ in **cm** and B in **days**, D comes out in **ha/cumec**.

### Step 6: Solve for Delta (Δ)

$$\boxed{\Delta = \dfrac{8.64 \, B}{D} \quad \text{(cm)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check:** For rice, B = 120 days, D = 800 ha/cumec → Δ = (8.64 × 120)/800 = **1.296 cm** of water per day over the season, ≈ 1555 mm total for the season. This matches the FAO water requirement for rice in Kerala (~1300–1600 mm).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Net Irrigation Requirement (NIR) and Field Irrigation Requirement (FIR)

### Step 1: Define the Field Water Balance

Over a cropping season of duration $T$ days, the water balance at the root zone is:

$$P + I + GW = ET_c + RO + DP \pm \Delta S$$

where:
- $P$ = Rainfall during the season (mm)
- $I$ = Irrigation applied (mm)
- $GW$ = Groundwater contribution through capillary rise (mm)
- $ET_c$ = Crop evapotranspiration (mm)
- $RO$ = Surface runoff (mm)
- $DP$ = Deep percolation below root zone (mm)
- $\Delta S$ = Change in root-zone soil water storage (mm)

### Step 2: Assume Steady-State Season (ΔS ≈ 0)

For a typical irrigation planning problem, the root zone is brought to Field Capacity at the start of the season and returns to FC at the end:

$$\Delta S \approx 0$$

### Step 3: Solve for Net Irrigation (I_net)

Rearranging:

$$I_{net} = ET_c + RO + DP - P - GW$$

### Step 4: Define Effective Rainfall (P_e)

Effective rainfall is the portion of total rainfall that is *useful* to the crop (infiltrates and is stored in the root zone). Empirical formulas include the USDA Soil Conservation Service (SCS) method:

$$P_e = \dfrac{P \times (125 - 0.2 P)}{125} \quad \text{for } P \leq 250 \text{ mm}$$

$$P_e = 125 + 0.1 P \quad \text{for } P > 250 \text{ mm}$$

For a typical planning problem, we take $I_{net} = ET_c - P_e$ (ignoring runoff, deep percolation, and groundwater for simplicity):

$$\boxed{NIR = ET_c - P_e = \sum_{i=1}^{T} K_{c,i} \cdot ETo_i - P_e}$$

### Step 5: Apply Field Application Efficiency

Real irrigation systems cannot deliver 100 % of water to the root zone; some is lost to deep percolation and runoff at the field:

$$FIR = \dfrac{NIR}{\eta_{application}}$$

### Step 6: Apply Conveyance Efficiency for the System

$$GIR = \dfrac{FIR}{\eta_{conveyance}} = \dfrac{NIR}{\eta_{application} \times \eta_{conveyance}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Example Efficiency Values:**
> - Surface (furrow/border): $\eta_{app} = 0.60$–$0.70$
> - Sprinkler: $\eta_{app} = 0.70$–$0.85$
> - Drip: $\eta_{app} = 0.90$–$0.95$
> - Unlined canal: $\eta_{con} = 0.60$–$0.70$
> - Lined canal: $\eta_{con} = 0.85$–$0.95$
> - Closed pipe: $\eta_{con} = 0.95$–$0.99$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Crop Coefficient Using Growing Degree Days (GDD) Approach

Some advanced KTU questions require deriving the Kc curve from thermal time:

### Step 1: Define Growing Degree Days (GDD)

$$GDD = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\dfrac{T_{max,i} + T_{min,i}}{2} - T_{base}\right)$$

where $T_{base}$ is the base temperature (e.g., 10 °C for wheat, 12 °C for maize).

### Step 2: Define Relative Thermal Time (RTT)

$$RTT = \dfrac{\sum GDD_{current}}{\sum GDD_{total}}$$

### Step 3: Build Kc Curve as a Function of RTT

The FAO-56 Kc curve is approximated by:

$$K_c(RTT) = \begin{cases} K_{c,ini}, & 0 \le RTT \le 0.10 \\ \dfrac{(K_{c,mid} - K_{c,ini})}{0.65} \cdot (RTT - 0.10) + K_{c,ini}, & 0.10 < RTT \le 0.75 \\ \dfrac{(K_{c,end} - K_{c,mid})}{0.25} \cdot (RTT - 0.75) + K_{c,mid}, & 0.75 < RTT \le 1.00 \end{cases}$$

This piecewise function is the *board-exam signature formula* for Kc.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Irrigation Interval (I.I.)

### Step 1: Net Depth to be Applied per Irrigation

$$d_{net} = \dfrac{RAW}{100} \times AWC \times d_{root} \times \dfrac{1}{\eta_{application}}$$

For *gross* application depth:

$$d_{gross} = \dfrac{RAW}{\eta_{application}}$$

where RAW is in mm and AWC is in mm/m.

### Step 2: Time Between Two Irrigations

If the crop uses water at the average rate of $ET_c$ mm/day, the number of days between two irrigations is:

$$\boxed{I.I. = \dfrac{d_{net}}{ET_c} \text{ days}}$$

### Step 3: Worked Example (Board Standard)

Given: AWC = 150 mm/m, root depth = 0.6 m, p = 0.5, $ET_c$ = 6 mm/day.
- Total AW = 150 × 0.6 = 90 mm
- RAW = 0.5 × 90 = 45 mm
- I.I. = 45 / 6 = **7.5 days** → irrigate every 7th or 8th day.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Leaching Requirement (LR) for Saline Soils

$$LR = \dfrac{EC_w}{5 \times EC_e - EC_w} \times 100 \quad (\%)$$

where:
- $EC_w$ = Electrical conductivity of irrigation water (dS/m)
- $EC_e$ = Electrical conductivity of soil saturation extract tolerated by the crop (dS/m)

**Example:** Wheat tolerates $EC_e$ = 6.0 dS/m. If $EC_w$ = 1.5 dS/m:

$$LR = \dfrac{1.5}{5 \times 6.0 - 1.5} \times 100 = \dfrac{1.5}{28.5} \times 100 \approx 5.26 \text{ \%}$$

This means 5.26 % *extra* water must be applied to leach salts below the root zone.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Critical Stages of Major Crops

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 — "Critical Stages Table" (Frequently Asked 7-Mark Question):**

> | Crop | Most Critical Stage for Water | Sensitivity Rank |
> |---|---|---|
> | Rice | Panicle initiation (PI) and flowering | Very High |
> | Wheat | Crown root (tillering) + booting + anthesis + grain filling | High |
> | Maize | Knee-high + tasselling + silking + grain filling | Very High |
> | Groundnut | Pegging + pod filling | High |
> | Cotton | Flowering + boll formation | High |
> | Sugarcane | Germination + tillering + grand growth | Moderate–High |
> | Pulses (Red gram) | Flowering + pod filling | Very High |
> | Coconut | Summer months (Feb–May in Kerala) | Continuous, no critical stage |
> | Banana | All stages, but fruit development | High |
> | Vegetables (Tomato) | Flowering + fruit set | Very High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Rhyming Memory Trick (Use in Exam):**
> **"P-F-G-S"** = Panicle initiation → Flowering → Grain filling → Senescence (last irrigation).

---

## 3.7 The LER (Land Equivalent Ratio) — Worked Numerical

**Example (KTU 2024 Model):** In a maize + cowpea intercropping system (1:1 row ratio), maize sole yield = 5000 kg/ha, cowpea sole yield = 1200 kg/ha. Intercropped yields: maize = 3000 kg/ha, cowpea = 800 kg/ha. Compute LER.

$$LER = \dfrac{Y_{maize,inter}}{Y_{maize,sole}} + \dfrac{Y_{cowpea,inter}}{Y_{cowpea,sole}}$$

$$LER = \dfrac{3000}{5000} + \dfrac{800}{1200}$$

$$LER = 0.60 + 0.667 = 1.267$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** LER = 1.267 means the intercropping system needs **26.7 % MORE land** under sole cropping to produce the same combined yield. Hence, intercropping is **biologically efficient by 26.7 %** and is recommended. LER > 1.0 → intercropping is advantageous.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
