# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — 3-MARK QUESTIONS (Short Answer)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model] Define cropping system. Differentiate it from cropping pattern.

**Model Answer:**

A **cropping system** is the totality of cropping activities and their management on a farm over time, including choice of crops, sequence, soil-water-fertilizer practices, and market linkages. A **cropping pattern**, in contrast, refers only to the *proportion of area* allocated to different crops in a given season or year.

> Cropping pattern is *what* is grown, whereas cropping system is *how, why, and with what* it is grown.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model] What is the difference between mixed cropping and intercropping? Give one example of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Mixed Cropping | Intercropping |
|---|---|---|
| Geometry | No row pattern | Definite row pattern |
| Example | Wheat + gram broadcast together | Maize + soybean in 1:2 row ratio |

Mixed cropping is purely an **insurance** strategy, whereas intercropping is a **yield-maximization** strategy.

---

## PART B — 14-MARK QUESTIONS (Module Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 MARKS

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the principles of an ideal crop rotation. Describe a suitable 3-year crop rotation for the Kuttanad region of Kerala.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define Crop Rotation:** Crop rotation is the practice of growing a series of different types of crops in the same area in sequenced seasons, following agronomic principles to sustain soil fertility and productivity.

**Step 2 — State the Principles (5 marks of sub-part):**
1. *Soil fertility principle:* Alternate heavy feeders (cereals) with light feeders / soil enrichers (legumes).
2. *Pest cycle disruption:* Avoid following a crop by another from the same family (e.g., rice → rice invites BPH and stem borer build-up).
3. *Root depth alternation:* Rotate shallow-rooted with deep-rooted crops.
4. *Moisture economy:* Include a dry-season crop if irrigation is scarce.
5. *Legume inclusion:* A legume must appear at least once in 2–3 years.
6. *Cover cropping:* A green-manure crop at least once in 3 years.
7. *Economic mix:* Alternate cash crop with food crop.

**Step 3 — Propose a 3-Year Kuttanad Rotation:**

| Year | Kharif (Jun–Oct) | Rabi (Nov–Mar) | Summer (Mar–May) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Year 1 | Rice (Punja) | Green gram / Daincha (in situ green manure) | Fallow |
| Year 2 | Rice (Kuruvai) | Sesame / Cowpea | Fallow |
| Year 3 | Rice (Third crop) | Pulses / Green manure | Fallow |

**Why this rotation works for Kuttanad:**
- Continuous rice is avoided → reduces *sheath blight, BPH, brown planthopper*.
- Green manure (*Sesbania rostrata*, *Daincha*) fixes 60–100 kg N/ha biologically.
- Sesame / pulses in rabi use residual moisture, breaking the rice monoculture.
- The rotation fits the hydrology of Kuttanad (a below-sea-level, flood-prone area in Kerala).

#### (b) [7 Marks] Define Crop Coefficient. With a neat sketch, describe the FAO-56 four-stage Kc curve. Mention typical Kc values for rice and banana at the mid-season stage.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define Kc (2 marks):**
$$K_c = \dfrac{ET_c}{ETo}$$

**Step 2 — Sketch the Four-Stage Curve (3 marks):** See Section 4.4 of these notes. Mention:
- Stage I (Initial): constant Kc_ini
- Stage II (Development): linearly increasing
- Stage III (Mid-season): peak plateau Kc_mid
- Stage IV (Late-season): declining Kc_end

**Step 3 — Typical Values (2 marks):**
- Rice (paddy, flooded): Kc_mid = **1.10 to 1.20** (because of evaporation from standing water + transpiration)
- Banana: Kc_mid = **1.00 to 1.10** (due to large leaf area index LAI > 4)

**Step 4 — One-line application:** Multiply Kc_mid by daily ETo (mm/day) to get daily ETc for the mid-season.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 MARKS

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Compute the consumptive use (Cu) of wheat grown in Thrissur using the Penman–Monteith method. Given: ETo for the 120-day base period averages 4.5 mm/day, Kc values are: initial 0.35 (20 days), development 0.75 (35 days), mid-season 1.15 (45 days), late-season 0.65 (20 days). Also compute the net irrigation requirement if effective rainfall (P_e) is 250 mm.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Cu for each stage (3 marks):**

| Stage | Kc | Days | ETo × Days (mm) | Cu = Kc × ETo × Days (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Initial | 0.35 | 20 | 4.5 × 20 = 90 | 0.35 × 90 = 31.5 |
| Development | 0.75 | 35 | 4.5 × 35 = 157.5 | 0.75 × 157.5 = 118.1 |
| Mid-season | 1.15 | 45 | 4.5 × 45 = 202.5 | 1.15 × 202.5 = 232.9 |
| Late-season | 0.65 | 20 | 4.5 × 20 = 90 | 0.65 × 90 = 58.5 |
| **Total** | — | **120** | **540** | **441.0 mm** |

$$\boxed{Cu = 441 \text{ mm} \text{ over the season}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Net Irrigation Requirement (2 marks):**

$$NIR = Cu - P_e = 441 - 250 = \boxed{191 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Field Irrigation Requirement (FIR) (1 mark):**

If $\eta_{application} = 0.70$ (surface irrigation):

$$FIR = \dfrac{191}{0.70} \approx 272.9 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Volume for 1 ha (1 mark):**

$$V = A \times FIR = 10{,}000 \text{ m}^2 \times 0.2729 \text{ m} = 2729 \text{ m}^3/\text{ha}$$

**Final Answer:** Total Cu = 441 mm, NIR = 191 mm, FIR = 272.9 mm, Volume = 2729 m³/ha.

#### (b) [7 Marks] List and explain the critical stages of irrigation for rice, wheat, and maize. Why is the flowering stage considered the most critical in most cereals?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Rice (2 marks):**
Critical stages: (i) Transplanting, (ii) Active tillering, (iii) Panicle initiation (PI), (iv) Anthesis (flowering), (v) Grain filling.
- Most critical: **Panicle initiation** — moisture stress at PI reduces effective tillers and grains/panicle, directly cutting yield.

**Step 2 — Wheat (2 marks):**
Critical stages (Crown Root Initiation = CRI is the *first and most critical*): (i) CRI at 20–25 DAS, (ii) Tillering, (iii) Jointing, (iv) Booting, (v) Anthesis, (vi) Milk stage.
- Most critical: **CRI + Anthesis** — these two together decide the final yield.

**Step 3 — Maize (2 marks):**
Critical stages: (i) Knee-high (V6–V8), (ii) Tasselling, (iii) Silking, (iv) Grain filling.
- Most critical: **Silking stage** — a 1-day moisture stress at silking can reduce yield by 6–8 %.

**Step 4 — Why Flowering is Most Critical (1 mark):**
At flowering:
- Pollen viability drops sharply under water stress.
- Anther dehiscence is impaired.
- Fertilization fails → empty grains / chaffy panicles.
- The plant cannot compensate later (no further floral primordia are produced).
- Even a brief stress (3–4 days) causes irreversible yield loss.

---

## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MISTAKES WHERE STUDENTS LOSE MARKS IN THIS MODULE:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Cropping Pattern" with "Cropping System"** — The pattern is *what* is grown, the system is *everything else*. Examiners deduct 2 marks if these are used interchangeably.
>
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency in Duty–Delta Derivation** — Always remember: Δ in **cm**, B in **days**, D in **ha/cumec**. Mixing cm with mm, or ha with acres, costs 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Mixing up FC and PWP values** — FC = **0.33 bar (1/3 bar)**, PWP = **15 bar**. Many students write 0.33 for PWP, which is wrong. Memorize the table.
>
> 4. **Not applying BOTH application and conveyance efficiencies** — NIR → FIR → GIR is a *three-step* sequence. Skipping a step loses 2 marks.
>
> 5. **Writing "Monoculture" for "Multiple Cropping"** — Monoculture means the *same crop year after year*; multiple cropping means *more than one crop per year*. These are opposites.
>
> 6. **Wrong interpretation of LER** — LER > 1 = intercropping is *advantageous* (NOT that yield is more). Students wrongly state "LER > 1 means yield is doubled" — false.
>
> 7. **Forgetting the Kc formula ETc = Kc × ETo** — Always write this as the first line whenever a question on crop water requirement appears.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST (For last-night KTU 2024 revision):**

- **Cropping Hierarchy:** Scheme → Pattern → System → Rotation → Multiple Cropping.
- **Cropping Intensity Formula:** $CI = (TCA/NSA) \times 100$.
- **Crop Rotation Principles:** 7 in number — fertility, pest, root, moisture, legume, cover, economic.
- **Mixed vs Inter vs Relay:** No geometry / Geometry / Time-overlap respectively.
- **LER > 1** → intercropping is biologically efficient.
- **Soil Water Constants:** Saturation (0 bar) → FC (0.33 bar, pF 2.54) → PWP (15 bar, pF 4.18) → Hygroscopic (31 bar) → Oven dry (pF 7).
- **AWC = FC − PWP** (per unit depth); **Total AW = AWC × ρb × d_root**.
- **Kc Curve:** 4 stages — Initial, Development, Mid-season, Late-season. Kc_mid for rice ≈ 1.20, wheat ≈ 1.15, maize ≈ 1.20, banana ≈ 1.10, coconut ≈ 1.00.
- **ETc = Kc × ETo** (the master equation).
- **NIR = ETc − P_e**; **FIR = NIR/η_app**; **GIR = FIR/η_con**.
- **Duty–Delta Formula:** $D = 8.64 B/\Delta$ (Δ in cm, B in days) or $\Delta = 8.64 B/D$.
- **Irrigation Interval:** $I.I. = RAW/ET_c$ in days.
- **Critical Stage Mnemonic:** **P-F-G-S** (Panicle initiation → Flowering → Grain filling → Senescence).
- **Most critical cereals:** Wheat (CRI), Rice (PI), Maize (Silking), Pulses (Flowering + pod-fill).
- **Crops needing continuous irrigation:** Coconut, Banana, Sugarcane, Vegetables.
- **Leaching Requirement:** $LR = EC_w / (5 EC_e - EC_w) \times 100$ %.
- **Effective Rainfall (SCS):** $P_e = P(125 - 0.2P)/125$ for P ≤ 250 mm.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weeds and Their Management in Crops
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT205 - CROP PRODUCTION & PROTECTION TECHNOLOGIES (AGRICULTURE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Weeds and Their Management in Crops

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Weed (KTU 2024 Definition):** A weed is any plant, whether agricultural, aquatic, or terrestrial, that grows in an unwanted location and competes with cultivated crops for resources such as light, water, nutrients, and space, thereby reducing crop yield, quality, and economic value.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, weeds are formally classified under the broader domain of **plant protection technologies** because they are the single most significant biotic constraint to crop production globally. The economic threshold of weed-related losses is estimated at **30–40%** of total agricultural productivity, surpassing losses from insect pests and plant diseases combined in many agro-ecosystems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Official KTU Terminology:** The syllabus explicitly categorizes weeds under "biotic stresses" along with insects, pathogens, and rodents. Students must use the term **"unwanted vegetation"** or **"volunteer plants"** in answer scripts when describing weed biology.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

Imagine you are hosting a dinner party for 20 guests, but uninvited people keep walking in, sitting at the table, eating the food, and taking up seats. Even though they are also "people" (just like the invited guests are people), they are unwelcome because they consume resources meant for your actual guests. The dinner suffers because there is not enough food, space, or attention for everyone.

**Weeds work exactly like this in a crop field:**
- The **crop plants** are the invited guests (rice, wheat, maize, etc.)
- The **weeds** are the uninvited intruders
- The **resources** are sunlight, water, soil nutrients, and space
- The **competition** reduces the harvest (yield) and quality

A more scientific analogy: think of weeds as **"biological noise"** in an agricultural system. Just as static noise interferes with a radio signal, weeds interfere with the clean transmission of solar energy, water, and nutrients to the intended crop.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Thresholds

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Quantitative Benchmarks (Must Memorize for KTU Exams):**
> - **Weed-related global crop loss:** 30–40% of potential yield
> - **Economic loss in India due to weeds:** ~₹1,00,000 crores annually
> - **Weed-crop competition critical period:** First 30–45 days after sowing (DAS)
> - **Weed seed bank in arable soil:** 10,000 to 1,00,000 seeds per m²
> - **Threshold of 1 weed per 10 m²** in row crops can reduce yield by 10%

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

This module carries significant weightage in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions. The KTU 2024 Scheme frequently tests:
1. Classification of weeds (botanical, ecological, life-cycle basis)
2. Differences between preventive and curative methods
3. Mode of action of herbicides
4. Integrated Weed Management (IWM) concepts
5. Calculations related to weed control efficiency and herbicide dosage
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Weeds (KTU High-Yield Topic)

### 2.1.1 Classification Based on Life Cycle

| Life Cycle Type | Definition | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Annuals** | Complete life cycle in one season | *Echinochloa crus-galli* (Barnyard grass) |
| **Biennials** | Complete life cycle in two seasons | *Daucus carota* (Wild carrot) |
| **Perennials** | Live for more than two years | *Cyperus rotundus* (Purple nutsedge) |

### 2.1.2 Classification Based on Botanical Family (Taxonomic)

| Family | Common Name | Example Species |
|---|---|---|
| **Poaceae (Gramineae)** | Grasses | *Echinochloa*, *Eleusine*, *Cynodon* |
| **Cyperaceae** | Sedges | *Cyperus rotundus*, *Cyperus iria* |
| **Asteraceae (Compositae)** | Broad-leaved | *Tridax*, *Parthenium* |
| **Fabaceae (Leguminosae)** | Legumes | *Lantana*, *Mimosa* |
| **Convolvulaceae** | Morning glories | *Ipomoea*, *Convolvulus* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** "**G**rasses are **P**oaceae, **S**edges are **C**yperaceae" — remember **GPS-C** for the three dominant monocot weed families in Indian agriculture.

### 2.1.3 Classification Based on Ecology

- **Agricultural weeds** — found in cultivated fields
- **Aquatic weeds** — found in water bodies (*Hydrilla*, *Eichhornia*)
- **Rangeland weeds** — found in pastures and grasslands
- **Forest weeds** — found in forest understory

### 2.1.4 Classification Based on Cotyledons

- **Monocot weeds** — narrow leaves, parallel venation (grasses, sedges)
- **Dicot weeds** — broad leaves, reticulate venation (most broadleaf weeds)

## 2.2 Characteristics of Weeds (Why Plants Become Weeds)

> [!NOTE]
> A plant is called a weed because of its **characteristics**, not its taxonomy. Any plant can be a weed if it grows in the wrong place.

**Key Adaptive Features of Weeds:**

1. **High reproductive capacity** — produce thousands of seeds per plant
2. **Seed dormancy** — seeds remain viable for many years (some up to 80 years)
3. **Rapid growth rate** — outcompete crops in early stages
4. **Allelopathy** — release toxic chemicals to suppress crop growth
5. **Seed dispersal mechanisms** — wind, water, animals, humans
6. **Vegetative propagation** — perennials reproduce via rhizomes, stolons, tubers
7. **C4 photosynthesis** — many weeds are C4 plants, highly efficient in high light/temperature

## 2.3 Harmful Effects of Weeds

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked "harmful effects of weeds," always structure the answer into **agronomic**, **economic**, **ecological**, and **health** sub-categories for full marks.

### 2.3.1 Agronomic Harms
- Competition for **light** → shading of crop seedlings
- Competition for **water** → drought stress
- Competition for **nutrients** → N, P, K depletion
- Competition for **space** → reduced plant population
- Release of **allelochemicals** → allelopathic suppression

### 2.3.2 Economic Harms
- Reduction in **crop yield** (up to 60–80% in severe infestation)
- Reduction in **crop quality** (mixing of weed seeds in grain)
- Increased **cost of cultivation** (extra labour, machinery, chemicals)
- **Parasitic weeds** like *Striga* and *Cuscuta* cause complete crop failure

### 2.3.3 Ecological Harms
- **Loss of biodiversity** — invasion by alien weeds (e.g., *Lantana camara*)
- **Water stagnation** — aquatic weeds block irrigation channels
- **Fire hazards** — dry weeds act as fuel
- **Soil erosion** — some invasive weeds alter soil structure

### 2.3.4 Health Harms
- **Pollen allergies** (e.g., *Parthenium hysterophorus*)
- **Toxicity to livestock** (e.g., *Lantana*, *Datura*)
- **Snake bites** — weeds harbor reptiles in unmanaged fields

## 2.4 Beneficial Effects of Weeds (Often Asked as Contrast)

| Beneficial Role | Example |
|---|---|
| Source of **medicines** | *Cynodon*, *Tridax* used in Ayurveda |
| **Soil conservation** | Weeds in non-cropped areas prevent erosion |
| **Fodder** | Some grasses are nutritious animal feed |
| **Indicator plants** | *Cyanodon* indicates alkaline soil |
| **Green manure** | Some legumes fix nitrogen |
| **Honey/bee plants** | Weeds support pollinator populations |
| **Genetic resources** | Wild relatives of crops |

## 2.5 Weed Management Methods (Core KTU Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5 Pillars of Weed Management (Must Memorize):**
> 1. **Preventive** — stopping weeds from entering
> 2. **Cultural** — using crop practices to suppress weeds
> 3. **Mechanical/Physical** — manual and machinery-based removal
> 4. **Biological** — using living organisms
> 5. **Chemical** — using herbicides

### 2.5.1 Preventive Weed Control

This is the **first line of defense** and the most economical method.

**Methods include:**
- **Use of clean/certified seed** — prevents introduction of weed seeds
- **Cleaning farm machinery** — before moving between fields
- **Cleaning irrigation water** — filters to remove weed seeds
- **Quarantine regulations** — preventing entry of alien invasive weeds
- **Manure composting** — proper composting kills weed seeds in FYM
- **Destroying weeds before flowering** — prevents seed production

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Point:** Preventive methods are **proactive** and **most cost-effective**, but they cannot eliminate existing weed problems.

### 2.5.2 Cultural Weed Control

These are **agronomic practices** that give crops a competitive advantage over weeds.

**Methods include:**
- **Crop rotation** — disrupts weed lifecycle
- **Inter-cropping / mixed cropping** — smothers weeds
- **Mulching** — physical barrier using straw, plastic films
- **Stale seedbed technique** — allows weeds to germinate, then killed before sowing
- **Sowing method** — line sowing allows inter-cultivation
- **Competitive crop varieties** — selecting tall, fast-growing varieties
- **Adjusting sowing time/density** — sowing at optimal time
- **Fertilizer placement** — placing fertilizer near crop row, not weeds
- **Flooding** — paddy fields

### 2.5.3 Mechanical/Physical Weed Control

The most ancient method, still widely used.

| Method | Description | Suitability |
|---|---|---|
| **Hand weeding** | Manual pulling or hoeing | Small areas, labour-intensive |
| **Hand hoe (Khurpi)** | Manual tool weeding | Row crops |
| **Animal-drawn implements** | Blade harrow, blade hoe | Small farms |
| **Power tillers / tractors** | Cultivator, rotavator | Large farms |
| **Inter-row cultivation** | Tine cultivator between rows | Wide-row crops |

**Advantages:**
- No chemical residue
- Effective in small holdings
- Improves soil aeration

**Disadvantages:**
- Labour-intensive and costly
- Not feasible in heavy rainfall
- May damage crop roots
- **Perennial weeds re-grow** from underground parts

### 2.5.4 Biological Weed Control

Using **living organisms** (insects, pathogens, fish, animals) to suppress weeds.

**Classical examples:**
- **Cactoblastis moth** controls *Opuntia* (prickly pear) in India
- **Zygogramma bicolorata** beetle controls *Parthenium hysterophorus*
- **Cypress bug** controls *Cyperus rotundus*
- **Fish (common carp)** controls aquatic weeds in paddy
- **Ducks** in paddy fields eat weeds
- **Goats** graze on weeds in non-cropped areas

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:** Biological control is **self-perpetuating** and **ecologically safe** but is **slow-acting** and **specific** to particular weed species.

### 2.5.5 Chemical Weed Control (Herbicides)

This is the **most rapidly growing** weed control method globally.

**Why herbicides are popular:**
- Quick action
- Labour-saving
- Effective against many weed species
- Suitable for large farms

**Classification of Herbicides (Multiple Criteria):**

#### A. Based on Selectivity

| Type | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Selective** | Kills specific weeds, spares crop | 2,4-D in wheat |
| **Non-selective** | Kills all vegetation | Glyphosate, Paraquat |

#### B. Based on Mode of Application

| Type | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Contact** | Kills only contacted plant parts | Paraquat |
| **Systemic (Translocated)** | Moves through plant system | Glyphosate, 2,4-D |

#### C. Based on Time of Application

| Type | When Applied | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Pre-plant incorporation (PPI)** | Mixed into soil before sowing | Trifluralin |
| **Pre-emergence (Pre-E)** | Before crop/weed emergence | Atrazine, Pendimethalin |
| **Post-emergence (Post-E)** | After emergence | 2,4-D, Glyphosate |

#### D. Based on Mode of Action (Site of Action)

| Site | Herbicide Examples | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Photosynthesis inhibitors** | Atrazine, Simazine | Block PS-II |
| **ALS inhibitors** | Sulfonylureas | Block amino acid synthesis |
| **EPSPS inhibitors** | Glyphosate | Block amino acid synthesis |
| **Cell division inhibitors** | Pendimethalin | Inhibit mitosis |
| **Growth regulators** | 2,4-D, MCPA | Disrupt hormone balance |
| **Lipid synthesis inhibitors** | Sethoxydim | Block fatty acid synthesis |

### 2.6 Integrated Weed Management (IWM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IWM Definition (KTU Standard):** The integration of two or more weed control methods (cultural, mechanical, biological, and chemical) in a complementary manner to manage weed populations below the economic threshold level (ETL), while minimizing environmental hazards.

**Principles of IWM:**
1. Use **multiple methods** rather than relying on one
2. **Rotate** herbicides to prevent resistance
3. Use **economic threshold** as decision criteria
4. **Monitor** weed populations regularly
5. **Prevent** weed seed production

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Formulas for KTU Numerical Problems (Memorize This Table):**

| Formula Name | Equation | Variables | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Weed Control Efficiency (WCE)** | $WCE = \frac{WC - WT}{WC} \times 100$ | $WC$ = weed count in control plot, $WT$ = weed count in treated plot | **%** | Evaluate herbicide efficacy |
| **Weed Index (WI)** | $WI = \frac{Yc - Yt}{Yc} \times 100$ | $Yc$ = yield in control, $Yt$ = yield in treated | **%** | Crop yield loss due to weeds |
| **Herbicide Dose Calculation** | $D = \frac{R \times A}{C}$ | $D$ = dose, $R$ = recommended rate, $A$ = area, $C$ = commercial product concentration | **kg or L** | Field application |
| **Spray Volume (Broadcast)** | $V = A \times S$ | $V$ = volume, $A$ = area, $S$ = spray solution rate | **L** | Sprayer calibration |
| **Weed Persistence Index** | $PI = \frac{Wt}{Wc}$ | $Wt$ = weed dry weight in treatment, $Wc$ = weed dry weight in control | **dimensionless** | Long-term herbicide effect |
| **Economic Threshold Level** | $ETL = \frac{Cc}{Pp \times Y \times P}$ | $Cc$ = control cost, $Pp$ = pest/weed density, $Y$ = yield loss per weed, $P$ = price | **weeds/m²** | Decision-making |
| **Critical Period of Weed Control (CPWC)** | $CPWC = t_1$ to $t_2$ | $t_1$ = days after sowing when control must begin, $t_2$ = days after which control can cease | **days** | Timing of weeding |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In all KTU examination scripts, never use the symbol `|` in mathematical expressions within tables. Always use `\vert` (in LaTeX) or the word "absolute" to denote modulus values.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Weed Control Efficiency (WCE) Formula

This is the most commonly asked numerical in KTU exams. We will derive the formula from first principles and then apply it to a typical exam problem.

### 3.1.1 The Underlying Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Why WCE exists:** When a farmer applies a herbicide, we need a **quantitative metric** to express how much the weed population was reduced compared to an untreated control plot. This metric must be a **percentage** so that results from different fields, crops, and years can be compared.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the baseline**

Let $WC$ be the number of weeds (per unit area) in the **control plot** (untreated). This represents the weed pressure if no control measure is applied.

**Step 2: Define the treated response**

Let $WT$ be the number of weeds (per unit area) in the **treated plot** where the weed control method is applied.

**Step 3: Calculate the absolute reduction**

The number of weeds eliminated by the treatment is:

$$Reduction = WC - WT$$

**Step 4: Convert to a fraction (relative reduction)**

Divide the reduction by the original (control) weed count to express it as a proportion:

$$Relative\ Reduction = \frac{WC - WT}{WC}$$

**Step 5: Convert to percentage**

Multiply by 100 to express as a percentage (this is the standard scientific convention):

$$WCE\ (\%) = \frac{WC - WT}{WC} \times 100$$

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical (Model KTU Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (Model 14-mark question):** In a weed control experiment on rice, the weed population in the control plot is 250 weeds/m², and the weed population in the herbicide-treated plot is 30 weeds/m². Calculate the Weed Control Efficiency.

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1: Identify the given values**

$$WC = 250\ \text{weeds/m}^2$$

$$WT = 30\ \text{weeds/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the WCE formula**

$$WCE = \frac{WC - WT}{WC} \times 100$$

**Step 3: Substitute the numerical values**

$$WCE = \frac{250 - 30}{250} \times 100$$

**Step 4: Calculate the numerator**

$$WCE = \frac{220}{250} \times 100$$

**Step 5: Perform the division**

$$WCE = 0.88 \times 100$$

**Step 6: Final answer**

$$WCE = 88\%$$

**Interpretation:** The herbicide treatment achieved **88% reduction** in weed population compared to the untreated control. This is considered **excellent** in field conditions (a WCE > 80% is classified as highly effective).

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Weed Index (WI)

The Weed Index is the **complementary metric** to WCE. While WCE measures weed reduction, WI measures **yield loss** caused by weeds.

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the maximum attainable yield**

Let $Yc$ = crop yield in the **weed-free control plot** (where weeds are completely removed manually). This represents the **maximum potential yield**.

**Step 2: Define the actual yield**

Let $Yt$ = crop yield in the **weed-infested plot** (where no weed control is done, or weeds are not removed properly).

**Step 3: Calculate the yield loss**

The yield loss due to weed competition is:

$$Yield\ Loss = Yc - Yt$$

**Step 4: Convert to relative loss (fraction)**

$$Relative\ Loss = \frac{Yc - Yt}{Yc}$$

**Step 5: Convert to percentage**

$$WI\ (\%) = \frac{Yc - Yt}{Yc} \times 100$$

### 3.2.2 Worked Numerical

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** In a wheat field, the yield in weed-free plot is 4,500 kg/ha, and the yield in weedy plot is 2,700 kg/ha. Calculate the Weed Index.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify values**

$$Yc = 4{,}500\ \text{kg/ha}$$

$$Yt = 2{,}700\ \text{kg/ha}$$

**Step 2: Apply the formula**

$$WI = \frac{4500 - 2700}{4500} \times 100$$

**Step 3: Numerator**

$$WI = \frac{1800}{4500} \times 100$$

**Step 4: Simplify**

$$WI = 0.4 \times 100$$

**Step 5: Final answer**

$$WI = 40\%$$

**Interpretation:** Weeds caused a **40% yield loss** in wheat. This is a typical value in unmanaged wheat fields and demonstrates why weed control is critical.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Herbicide Dose Calculation

### 3.3.1 The Problem Context

Herbicides are sold as **commercial formulations** (e.g., 38% EC, 75% WP) — meaning the active ingredient is mixed with inert carriers. A farmer must calculate how much commercial product to buy for a given field area.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify the recommended rate (R)**

This is the dose recommended by the manufacturer, expressed in **kg of active ingredient (a.i.) per hectare** or **L per hectare**.

**Step 2: Identify the commercial concentration (C)**

This is the percentage of active ingredient in the commercial product. For example, a 38% EC has $C = 38$.

**Step 3: Identify the field area (A)**

The total area to be treated, in hectares.

**Step 4: Derive the formula**

The total active ingredient needed is:

$$AI\ needed = R \times A$$

But the commercial product contains only a fraction $C/100$ of active ingredient. Therefore, the amount of commercial product (D) is:

$$D = \frac{AI\ needed}{C/100}$$

Simplifying:

$$D = \frac{R \times A \times 100}{C}$$

Equivalently:

$$D = \frac{R \times A}{C/100}$$

**Step 5: Units check**

$$D\ (kg\ or\ L) = \frac{(kg/ha \times ha) \times 100}{(\%)} = \text{kg or L of commercial product}$$

### 3.3.3 Worked Numerical (High-Yield KTU Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** The recommended dose of Pendimethalin is 1.0 kg a.i./ha. The commercial product is 30% EC. Calculate the quantity of commercial product required for 2 hectares.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify values**

$$R = 1.0\ \text{kg a.i./ha}$$

$$C = 30\%$$

$$A = 2\ \text{ha}$$

**Step 2: Apply the formula**

$$D = \frac{R \times A}{C/100}$$

**Step 3: Substitute values**

$$D = \frac{1.0 \times 2}{30/100}$$

**Step 4: Simplify the denominator**

$$D = \frac{2}{0.30}$$

**Step 5: Final answer**

$$D = 6.67\ \text{L of commercial product}$$

**Interpretation:** The farmer needs **6.67 liters** of Pendimethalin 30% EC to treat 2 hectares.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Weed Management Methods

> [!NOTE]
> This comparison matrix is a **high-yield table** that KTU examiners frequently ask students to reproduce. Memorize the structure for full marks.

| Parameter | Preventive | Cultural | Mechanical | Biological | Chemical |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cost** | Very low | Low | High (labour) | Low | Medium |
| **Effectiveness** | Low (preventive only) | Medium | High (annuals) | Slow, partial | High |
| **Speed of action** | Pre-event | Slow | Immediate | Very slow | Fast |
| **Sustainability** | High | High | Medium | High | Low (resistance) |
| **Perennial weed control** | None | Poor | Poor (re-growth) | Selective | Excellent (systemic) |
| **Selectivity** | None | Crop-specific | Non-selective | Species-specific | Selective/Non-selective |
| **Skill required** | Low | Medium | High | High | High |
| **Environmental impact** | None | Positive | Low | Positive | High (residue) |
| **Best suited for** | All farms | All farms | Small/medium farms | Large monoculture | Large commercial farms |

## 3.5 Critical Period of Weed Control (CPWC) — Analytical Derivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concept:** The CPWC is the time interval during the crop growth period when weed competition causes **maximum yield loss**, and weed removal during this period is most critical.

**Conceptual Framework:**

The crop-weed competition follows a **bell-shaped curve**. The CPWC can be determined by two experiments:

**Experiment 1: Weeds allowed to grow for increasing durations (X-axis = weeks after sowing, Y-axis = yield loss %)**

This gives the **upper limit** of CPWC — i.e., if weeds are allowed to grow beyond this time, the yield loss is unacceptably high.

**Experiment 2: Weeds removed at increasing durations (X-axis = weeks after sowing, Y-axis = yield loss %)**

This gives the **lower limit** of CPWC — i.e., if weeds are removed before this time, the yield is fully protected.

**The CPWC is the overlap of these two curves.**

Mathematically:

$$CPWC = [t_1, t_2]$$

where $t_1$ = **earliest time weeds must be removed** (from Experiment 1), and $t_2$ = **latest time weeds can be allowed to grow** (from Experiment 2).

**Typical CPWC values for major crops (Must memorize):**

| Crop | CPWC (DAS) |
|---|---|
| Rice (transplanted) | 15–45 DAS |
| Rice (direct seeded) | 10–40 DAS |
| Wheat | 30–45 DAS |
| Maize | 15–45 DAS |
| Soybean | 15–30 DAS |
| Cotton | 30–60 DAS |
| Groundnut | 20–45 DAS |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Warning:** Many students write "critical period of competition" as a single number. The correct answer is always a **range** (e.g., 30–45 days, not 45 days).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define a weed. List any four characteristics that enable weeds to survive in agricultural fields.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A weed is any plant growing in a place where it is not desired, and which interferes with the growth and yield of cultivated crops by competing for resources like light, water, nutrients, and space.

**Four characteristics of weeds:**

1. **High seed production** — A single weed plant can produce thousands of seeds (e.g., *Chenopodium* produces up to 70,000 seeds per plant).
2. **Seed dormancy** — Weed seeds remain viable in the soil for many years, forming a persistent seed bank.
3. **Rapid vegetative growth** — Weeds grow faster than crops, giving them a competitive edge.
4. **Allelopathy** — Many weeds release toxic biochemicals (allelochemicals) that suppress the germination and growth of nearby crop plants (e.g., *Parthenium hysterophorus* releases parthenin).

*(Plus any one of the following for full marks: efficient seed dispersal, C4 photosynthesis, ability to grow in adverse conditions, vegetative reproduction in perennials.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between selective and non-selective herbicides with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Selective Herbicide | Non-Selective Herbicide |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Kills specific weed species without harming the crop | Kills all vegetation it contacts |
| **Action mechanism** | Selectively toxic to certain plant biochemical pathways | Non-specific destruction of plant tissues |
| **Selectivity basis** | Differential metabolism, morphology, or anatomy of plants | Toxic to all green plants |
| **Application stage** | Generally post-emergence in standing crop | Used in non-crop areas or before sowing |
| **Example** | 2,4-D (kills broadleaf weeds in wheat) | Glyphosate, Paraquat |
| **Safety** | Safe to crop if used at recommended dose | Must be used carefully to avoid crop damage |

---

## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Classify weeds based on **life cycle** and **ecology** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **mechanical and biological methods** of weed control in detail. **(7 marks)**

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **classification of herbicides** based on selectivity, mode of action, and time of application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **Integrated Weed Management (IWM)**? Discuss its components and advantages. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A (a) (7 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification of Weeds Based on Life Cycle:**

**1. Annual Weeds:** Complete their life cycle in one season or one year. They reproduce only by seeds.
- **Examples:** *Echinochloa crus-galli* (barnyard grass), *Amaranthus viridis* (pigweed), *Chenopodium album* (lamb's quarter)

**2. Biennial Weeds:** Complete their life cycle in two years. Vegetative growth in the first year; flowering and seed production in the second year.
- **Examples:** *Daucus carota* (wild carrot), *Trachyspermum*

**3. Perennial Weeds:** Live for more than two years. Reproduce by seeds as well as vegetative parts (rhizomes, tubers, stolons, bulbs).
- **Examples:** *Cyperus rotundus* (purple nutsedge), *Cynodon dactylon* (Bermuda grass), *Sorghum halepense* (Johnson grass)

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification of Weeds Based on Ecology:**

**1. Agricultural (Terrestrial) Weeds:** Found in cultivated fields and compete with crops.
- **Examples:** *Echinochloa*, *Cyperus*, *Parthenium*

**2. Aquatic Weeds:** Grow in water bodies — ponds, lakes, canals, rice fields.
- **Examples:** *Eichhornia crassipes* (water hyacinth), *Hydrilla*, *Pistia*

**3. Rangeland/Pasture Weeds:** Found in grasslands and pastures.
- **Examples:** *Lantana camara*, *Xanthium*

**4. Forest Weeds:** Found in forest areas and plantations.
- **Examples:** *Lantana*, *Mikania*

**5. Waste-land Weeds:** Grow in wastelands, roadsides, and disturbed areas.
- **Examples:** *Parthenium hysterophorus*, *Calotropis*

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A (b) (7 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mechanical/Physical Methods of Weed Control:**

Mechanical weed control involves the use of **manual tools, animal-drawn implements, or power-driven machinery** to physically remove or destroy weeds.

**Methods:**

1. **Hand weeding** — Manual pulling or removal of weeds by hand. Most effective for annual weeds in small areas; not feasible for large farms.

2. **Hand hoeing** — Using hand tools like *khurpi* or spade to cut and uproot weeds. Effective in row crops.

3. **Animal-drawn implements** — Traditional tools like blade harrow and spike-tooth harrow pulled by bullocks. Still used in small farms.

4. **Tractor/power-tiller operated implements** — Cultivator, rotavator, and disc harrow for large-scale weed control in line-sown crops.

5. **Inter-row cultivation** — Using tine cultivators to remove weeds between crop rows without damaging the crop.

6. **Mowing/slashing** — Cutting weeds above ground level; useful in non-crop areas.

7. **Burning** — Controlled burning of weeds in stubble fields; useful for parasitic weeds.

**Advantages:** No chemical residue, improves soil aeration, suitable for organic farming.
**Disadvantages:** Labour-intensive, not effective for perennial weeds (regrowth from underground parts).

> [!NOTE]
> **Biological Methods of Weed Control:**

Biological control uses **living organisms** (insects, pathogens, fish, animals) to suppress weed populations. It is **species-specific** and **ecologically safe**.

**Examples:**

1. **Cactoblastis cactorum** moth — controls *Opuntia* (prickly pear cactus) in India.
2. **Zygogramma bicolorata** beetle — controls *Parthenium hysterophorus* (congress grass).
3. **Neochetina eichhorniae** weevil — controls *Eichhornia crassipes* (water hyacinth).
4. **Common carp fish** — controls aquatic weeds in rice fields.
5. **Ducks and geese** — graze on weeds in paddy fields.
6. **Goats and sheep** — graze on weeds in non-cropped land and plantations.
7. **Fungal pathogens** — *Puccinia* species for *Cyperus* control.

**Advantages:** Self-perpetuating, low cost, no pollution.
**Disadvantages:** Slow action, species-specific, requires research.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B (a) (7 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification of Herbicides:**

**A. Based on Selectivity:**

1. **Selective herbicides** — Kill specific weeds while sparing the crop. Example: **2,4-D** kills broadleaf weeds in cereal crops like wheat.
2. **Non-selective herbicides** — Kill all plants, used in non-crop areas. Example: **Glyphosate** used before sowing or in wasteland.

**B. Based on Mode of Action (Site of Action):**

1. **Photosynthesis inhibitors (PS II)** — Block electron transport in chloroplast. Example: **Atrazine, Simazine**
2. **Amino acid synthesis inhibitors (EPSPS)** — Block shikimic acid pathway. Example: **Glyphosate**
3. **Auxin-mimics / Growth regulators** — Disrupt hormone balance, cause uncontrolled growth. Example: **2,4-D, MCPA**
4. **Cell division inhibitors** — Block mitosis and root/shoot growth. Example: **Pendimethalin, Trifluralin**
5. **ALS inhibitors** — Block acetolactate synthase. Example: **Sulfonylureas** (Metsulfuron)

**C. Based on Time of Application:**

1. **Pre-plant incorporation (PPI)** — Mixed into soil before sowing crop. Example: **Trifluralin** (volatile, needs incorporation)
2. **Pre-emergence (Pre-E)** — Applied to soil before crop/weed emergence. Example: **Atrazine** in maize, **Pendimethalin** in rice
3. **Post-emergence (Post-E)** — Applied after emergence of weeds. Example: **2,4-D** in wheat, **Glyphosate** in non-crop areas

**Additional classifications:**
- **Contact** (Paraquat) vs **Systemic** (Glyphosate)
- **Soil-applied** vs **Foliage-applied**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B (b) (7 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Integrated Weed Management (IWM) — Definition:**

IWM is the **systematic integration of two or more weed control methods** (cultural, mechanical, biological, and chemical) in a compatible manner to maintain weed populations **below the economic threshold level (ETL)**, while minimizing environmental degradation and ensuring sustainable crop production.

**Components of IWM:**

1. **Preventive measures** — Clean seed, machinery sanitation, quarantine
2. **Cultural practices** — Crop rotation, intercropping, mulching, stale seedbed, competitive varieties
3. **Mechanical methods** — Hand weeding, inter-row cultivation, power tillage
4. **Biological control** — Insects, pathogens, fish, livestock
5. **Chemical control** — Selective herbicides at recommended doses
6. **Herbicide rotation** — Alternating herbicides with different modes of action to prevent resistance
7. **Monitoring and threshold-based decisions** — Scouting, weed mapping, ETL calculation

**Advantages of IWM:**

1. **Sustainable** — Reduces dependence on a single method
2. **Prevents herbicide resistance** — Rotation delays resistance development
3. **Environmentally safe** — Reduces chemical load on environment
4. **Cost-effective** — Combines low-cost methods with effective ones
5. **Effective against diverse weeds** — Targets annuals, biennials, and perennials
6. **Improves soil health** — Less chemical contamination
7. **Long-term weed suppression** — Reduces weed seed bank over time

**Example:** In rice cultivation, IWM may include (1) certified seed, (2) stale seedbed, (3) transplanting 21-day-old seedlings, (4) one hand weeding at 25 DAT, (5) application of Bispyribac-sodium (post-emergence) at 25–30 DAT, and (6) alternate wetting and drying water management.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> **1. Confusing CPWC:** Writing the critical period as a single number (e.g., "30 days") instead of a range (e.g., "30–45 DAS"). This is a **1–2 mark deduction** by strict KTU valuators.
> 
> **2. Mixing up herbicide classifications:** Students often confuse **"selective"** with **"systemic"** — these are TWO DIFFERENT criteria. Selective vs Non-selective is based on *which plants* are killed. Contact vs Systemic is based on *how* the herbicide moves. KTU expects a clear table.
> 
> **3. No examples in classification answers:** Always provide **2 examples per category** in weed classification answers. Without examples, the answer is considered incomplete (loses 2–3 marks).
> 
> **4. WCE and WI formula confusion:** Students interchange the formulas. Remember: **WCE = weed reduction**; **WI = yield loss**. They are **inverse** in concept.
> 
> **5. Forgetting units:** WCE and WI are expressed in **%**; herbicide dose in **kg/ha** or **L/ha**; CPWC in **DAS** (Days After Sowing). Missing units = **0.5–1 mark deduction**.
> 
> **6. IWM without components:** Simply writing "IWM combines many methods" is insufficient. List **at least 4–5 components** explicitly for full marks.
> 
> **7. Confusing pre-emergence with pre-plant incorporation:** PPI is **mixed into soil** before sowing; Pre-emergence is **sprayed on soil surface** after sowing but before emergence.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — WEEDS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT**

**Core Definitions:**
- [ ] Weed = unwanted plant in cultivated area
- [ ] ETL = Economic Threshold Level
- [ ] CPWC = Critical Period of Weed Control (a RANGE, not a single number)
- [ ] IWM = Integrated Weed Management
- [ ] Allelopathy = release of biochemicals to suppress other plants

**Classification Mastery (Memorize Examples):**
- [ ] Annual: *Echinochloa*, *Amaranthus*
- [ ] Biennial: *Daucus carota*
- [ ] Perennial: *Cyperus rotundus*, *Cynodon dactylon*
- [ ] Monocot: grasses, sedges (Poaceae, Cyperaceae)
- [ ] Dicot: broad-leaved weeds
- [ ] Aquatic: *Eichhornia*, *Hydrilla*
- [ ] Parasitic: *Striga*, *Cuscuta*

**5 Methods of Weed Management:**
- [ ] Preventive — clean seed, machinery, quarantine
- [ ] Cultural — rotation, intercropping, mulching, stale seedbed
- [ ] Mechanical — hand weeding, hoeing, tractor cultivation
- [ ] Biological — Cactoblastis (Opuntia), Zygogramma (Parthenium)
- [ ] Chemical — herbicides

**Herbicide Classification:**
- [ ] Selective (2,4-D) vs Non-selective (Glyphosate)
- [ ] Contact (Paraquat) vs Systemic (Glyphosate)
- [ ] Pre-plant (Trifluralin), Pre-emergence (Atrazine), Post-emergence (2,4-D)
- [ ] PS-II inhibitors (Atrazine), EPSPS (Glyphosate), Auxin mimics (2,4-D)

**Key Formulas (Must Memorize):**
- [ ] WCE (%) = (WC - WT) / WC × 100
- [ ] WI (%) = (Yc - Yt) / Yc × 100
- [ ] Herbicide dose D = (R × A) / (C/100)

**Standard Metrics:**
- [ ] Weed losses: 30–40% globally
- [ ] CPWC for rice: 15–45 DAT (transplanted)
- [ ] CPWC for wheat: 30–45 DAS
- [ ] Weed seed bank: 10,000 to 1,00,000 seeds/m²
- [ ] 1 weed per 10 m² = 10% yield loss (rule of thumb)

**IWM Components:**
- [ ] Preventive + Cultural + Mechanical + Biological + Chemical
- [ ] Herbicide rotation to prevent resistance
- [ ] ETL-based decision making
- [ ] Long-term weed seed bank reduction

**Biological Control Champions (Remember These Three):**
- [ ] *Cactoblastis cactorum* → *Opuntia* (prickly pear)
- [ ] *Zygogramma bicolorata* → *Parthenium* (congress grass)
- [ ] *Neochetina eichhorniae* → *Eichhornia* (water hyacinth)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time and Method of Sowing of Major Field Crops, Seed Rate for Important Crops
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT205 - CROP PRODUCTION & PROTECTION TECHNOLOGIES (AGRICULTURE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Time and Method of Sowing of Major Field Crops, Seed Rate for Important Crops

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🌾 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Sowing — The Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Sowing** is the precise agricultural operation of placing seeds into the prepared soil at a controlled **depth**, **spacing**, and **time**, so that they germinate, emerge, and establish a healthy crop stand. For KTU examinations, sowing is one of the six major crop production operations: **Tillage → Sowing → Irrigation → Manuring → Weeding → Harvesting**.

Sowing is the **single most critical determinant** of crop yield because:
- It determines the **plant population per unit area** (the basic source of yield).
- It fixes the **germination environment** (moisture, temperature, depth).
- It dictates the **crop geometry** (row-to-row and plant-to-plant spacing) which governs light interception and nutrient uptake.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Theatre Seating" Model

Think of a farmer's field as a **theatre auditorium**:
- Every seed is a **seat reservation** for a future plant (the "audience member").
- The farmer wants **every seat filled** — no empty seat (poor yield), no overcrowding (competition for water, light, nutrients).
- The **sowing time** is like the "show timing" — too early, the audience (seeds) may not turn up because the theatre is too cold; too late, the show clashes with another performance.
- The **sowing method** is the **usher** who places each ticket-holder (seed) at the correct seat with proper distance.
- The **seed rate** is the **number of tickets printed** — print too many and you waste money on empty seats; print too few and you lose revenue.

Just as a smart theatre manager times the show and places audience members correctly, a smart farmer times sowing and places seeds correctly.

## 1.3 Three Pillars of Successful Sowing

| Pillar | Definition | Key Metric |
|--------|------------|------------|
| **Time of Sowing** | The optimum calendar window determined by monsoon, temperature, photoperiod | Sowing date / week number |
| **Method of Sowing** | The physical technique used to place seeds in soil | Broadcasting, Drilling, Dibbling, Transplanting, etc. |
| **Seed Rate** | Quantity of seed sown per unit area to achieve desired plant population | kg/ha or kg/acre |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic carries a **minimum 7-mark weightage** in End Semester Examinations under Module 2, often combined with irrigation or manuring in a 14-mark Module-2 question. Mastering seed rate numericals is essential.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Time of Sowing — The Indian Cropping Calendar

India's agriculture is divided into three major cropping seasons. The KTU board **loves** asking "Classify the following crops as Kharif/Rabi/Zaid" — this is a 3-marker favourite.

### 🌧️ A. Kharif Season (Monsoon Crops)
- **Sowing Period:** June – July (with the onset of South-West Monsoon)
- **Harvesting:** September – October
- **Crops:** Rice, Maize, Sorghum (Jowar), Pearl Millet (Bajra), Cotton, Groundnut, Soybean, Pigeon pea (Tur), Black gram, Green gram, Sesame

### ❄️ B. Rabi Season (Winter Crops)
- **Sowing Period:** October – November
- **Harvesting:** March – April
- **Crops:** Wheat, Barley, Gram (Chana), Mustard, Linseed, Safflower, Field pea, Lentil

### ☀️ C. Zaid Season (Summer Crops)
- **Sowing Period:** March – April
- **Harvesting:** May – June
- **Crops:** Watermelon, Muskmelon, Cucumber, Ridge gourd, Fodder maize, Sorghum (fodder)

> [!NOTE]
> **Scientific Basis of Sowing Time:** The sowing time is governed by **temperature**, **rainfall**, **photoperiod (day length)**, and **soil moisture availability**. For example, wheat requires cool temperatures (10–15 °C) for tillering — hence winter sowing; rice requires warm, humid conditions (25–30 °C) — hence monsoon sowing.

---

## 2.2 Methods of Sowing — Complete Classification

### 🔹 2.2.1 Broadcasting
- **Process:** Seeds are scattered manually or mechanically (broadcasters) uniformly over the prepared field surface.
- **Suitable Crops:** Wheat (small seeds), Mustard, Linseed, Sesamum, Fodder crops
- **Advantages:** Quick, cheap, requires no machinery for small areas
- **Disadvantages:** Uneven depth placement, poor seed-to-soil contact, uneven plant stand, wastage of seed

### 🔹 2.2.2 Drilling (Line Sowing)
- **Process:** Seeds are dropped in furrows at uniform depth and spacing using a seed drill, dibbler, or planter.
- **Suitable Crops:** Wheat, Maize, Sorghum, Cotton, Groundnut, Soybean
- **Row Spacing:** 20–45 cm (cereal) to 60–90 cm (cotton, maize)
- **Advantages:** Uniform depth, proper plant population, facilitates intercultural operations (weeding, hoeing), saves seed (15–20% less than broadcasting)
- **Disadvantages:** Higher initial cost, requires trained operator

### 🔹 2.2.3 Dibbling
- **Process:** Seeds are placed in small holes (dibbles) made at proper spacing with a dibbler (hand tool). 2–3 seeds per hole.
- **Suitable Crops:** Cotton, Sorghum, Pigeon pea, Vegetables (hybrid seeds), transplanted crops
- **Advantages:** Most precise method, saves seed, ideal for hybrids and bold seeds
- **Disadvantages:** Labour-intensive, time-consuming

### 🔹 2.2.4 Transplanting
- **Process:** Seeds are first raised in a nursery bed (3–6 weeks) and then seedlings are transplanted to the main field.
- **Suitable Crops:** Rice (paddy), Tomato, Brinjal, Chilli, Onion, Tobacco
- **Advantages:** Better crop establishment, efficient use of land (nursery area is small), better weed control in main field
- **Disadvantages:** Labour-intensive, requires puddling in rice, transplanting shock

### 🔹 2.2.5 Kera / Pora Method (Seed dropping behind plough)
- **Process:** A skilled person drops seeds by hand into the furrow opened by a country plough (bullock or tractor drawn).
- **Suitable Crops:** Wheat, Gram, Pea, Linseed in rain-fed areas
- **Advantages:** Low-cost, traditional, suitable for small farms
- **Disadvantages:** Slow, uneven seed placement

### 🔹 2.2.6 Hill Dropping & Check Row Planting
- **Process:** Seeds are dropped at fixed "hills" (small clusters) at precise row-to-row and plant-to-plant spacing using a check-row planter.
- **Suitable Crops:** Maize, Cotton, Groundnut
- **Advantages:** Allows cross-cultivation in both directions (interculture)
- **Disadvantages:** Slow operation

### 🔹 2.2.7 Zero Tillage / No-Till Sowing
- **Process:** Sowing is done directly into the residue of previous crop without tillage, using a zero-till drill.
- **Suitable Crops:** Wheat after rice, Rabi crops after Kharif
- **Advantages:** Saves fuel, time, water; prevents soil erosion; suited for rice-wheat system of Indo-Gangetic plains
- **Disadvantages:** Requires special machinery, weed management is critical

---

## 2.3 Seed Rate — Definition, Significance & Formula Sheet

### 🔹 Definition
> **Seed rate** is the quantity of seed required to sow a unit area of land (kg/ha) to obtain the desired plant population after accounting for germination percentage and purity percentage of the seed lot.

### 🔹 Why Seed Rate is Critical
- **Under-seeding** → Low plant population → Low yield
- **Over-seeding** → High competition → Wasted seed, lodging, disease

### 📐 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Unit | Remarks |
|---|--------------------|-----------------------|------|---------|
| 1 | **Pure Live Seed (PLS)** | $\text{PLS\%} = \text{Germination\%} \times \text{Purity\%}$ | % | Both expressed as decimals or % together |
| 2 | **Seed Rate (general)** | $\text{SR} = \dfrac{\text{Seeds/m}^2 \times 1000\text{-seed wt (g)} \times 10{,}000}{1000 \times \text{Germination\%} \times \text{Purity\%}}$ | kg/ha | Standard KTU numerical formula |
| 3 | **Simplified Seed Rate** | $\text{SR (kg/ha)} = \dfrac{\text{Seeds/m}^2 \times 1000\text{-seed wt (g)} \times 100}{\text{Germination\%} \times \text{Purity\%}}$ | kg/ha | When G% and P% are in % form |
| 4 | **Seeds/m² to Seeds/ha** | $\text{Seeds/ha} = \text{Seeds/m}^2 \times 10{,}000$ | seeds/ha | 1 ha = 10,000 m² |
| 5 | **Actual Seeds Required (raw)** | $\text{Seeds/ha (raw)} = \dfrac{\text{Desired seeds/ha}}{\text{PLS\%}}$ | seeds/ha | To compensate for dead/dormant seeds |
| 6 | **Test Weight** | Weight of 1000 seeds | grams (g) | Crop-specific; given in tables |
| 7 | **Spacing formula** | $\text{Row spacing} \times \text{Plant spacing} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{\text{Plant pop./ha}}$ | cm² | Geometric arrangement |

### 🔹 Standard Seed Rate Reference Table for Major Crops

| Crop | Method of Sowing | Sowing Time | Seed Rate (kg/ha) | 1000-seed wt (g) |
|------|------------------|-------------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Rice (transplanted)** | Transplanting (nursery) | June–July | 20–25 | 22–25 |
| **Rice (direct seeded)** | Drilling / Broadcasting | June | 60–80 | 22–25 |
| **Wheat (irrigated)** | Drilling / Broadcasting | Oct–Nov | 100–125 | 38–42 |
| **Wheat (rain-fed)** | Broadcasting / Drilling | Oct–Nov | 90–100 | 38–42 |
| **Maize (composite)** | Dibbling / Drilling | June–July | 18–20 | 250–300 |
| **Maize (hybrid)** | Dibbling | June–July | 15–20 | 250–300 |
| **Sorghum (Jowar)** | Drilling / Dibbling | June–July | 8–10 | 25–30 |
| **Pearl Millet (Bajra)** | Drilling | July | 4–5 | 7–9 |
| **Cotton (hybrid)** | Dibbling | May–June | 15–20 | 90–100 |
| **Cotton (variety)** | Dibbling | May–June | 20–25 | 90–100 |
| **Groundnut** | Drilling / Dibbling | May–June | 80–100 | 350–500 |
| **Soybean** | Drilling | June–July | 55–65 | 130–150 |
| **Pigeon pea (Tur)** | Dibbling | June–July | 12–15 | 60–80 |
| **Sugarcane (sett rate)** | Furrow planting | Feb–Mar / Oct–Nov | 35,000–40,000 three-budded setts/ha | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Board:** Whenever the question mentions "hybrid" seed, the seed rate is **lower** (e.g., hybrid maize = 15–20 kg/ha vs composite = 18–25 kg/ha) because hybrids have **higher vigour, germination, and yield potential**. Composite/variety seeds need a higher seed rate.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Seed Rate Formula

### 🎯 Objective
To derive the universal **Seed Rate (kg/ha)** formula used in KTU numerical problems.

### 📋 Given Quantities
- **N** = Desired plant population per hectare (plants/ha)
- **W** = Test weight / 1000-seed weight (grams)
- **G%** = Germination percentage of seed lot (e.g., 85)
- **P%** = Physical purity percentage of seed lot (e.g., 98)

### 🔢 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert desired plant population to seeds per square metre**

Since **1 hectare = 10,000 m²**:

$$
N_{\text{m}^2} = \dfrac{N}{10{,}000} \quad \text{[seeds per m}^2\text{]}
$$

*Explanation:* We divide the per-hectare population by 10,000 to get the per-m² value.

**Step 2 — Account for Germination loss**

Not every seed germinates. Only **G% of G/100** seeds will produce a seedling. So we need to sow **more** raw seeds.

$$
\text{Seeds to be sown per m}^2 = \dfrac{N_{\text{m}^2}}{G/100} = \dfrac{N_{\text{m}^2} \times 100}{G}
$$

*Explanation:* If you want 50 seedlings/m² and only 80% germinate, you must sow $50/0.80 = 62.5$ raw seeds/m².

**Step 3 — Account for Purity loss**

The seed lot contains inert matter (chaff, broken seeds, weed seeds). Only **P% of P/100** seeds are "pure live seeds". So we need to sow even more.

$$
\text{Raw seeds to be sown per m}^2 = \dfrac{N_{\text{m}^2} \times 100}{G \times P/100} = \dfrac{N_{\text{m}^2} \times 10{,}000}{G \times P}
$$

*Explanation:* If purity is 98%, then 2% of bag weight is waste. To get 62.5 live seeds/m², you need $62.5 / 0.98 = 63.78$ raw seeds/m².

**Step 4 — Convert seeds to weight (using Test Weight)**

The **test weight W** is the weight (in grams) of 1000 seeds. Therefore, **1 seed weighs W/1000 grams**.

$$
\text{Weight of raw seeds per m}^2 \text{ (g)} = \dfrac{N_{\text{m}^2} \times 10{,}000}{G \times P} \times \dfrac{W}{1000}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert g/m² to kg/ha**

Multiplying by $10{,}000 \text{ m}^2/\text{ha}$ to scale up, and dividing by $1000 \text{ g/kg}$ to convert to kg:

$$
\text{Seed Rate (kg/ha)} = \dfrac{N_{\text{m}^2} \times 10{,}000}{G \times P} \times \dfrac{W}{1000} \times \dfrac{10{,}000}{1000}
$$

**Step 6 — Simplification (Substitute $N_{\text{m}^2} = N/10{,}000$)**

$$
\text{Seed Rate (kg/ha)} = \dfrac{N \times W \times 10{,}000}{G \times P \times 1000 \times 10{,}000 \times 1000}
$$

After cancellation and rearranging, the elegant final form:

$$
\boxed{\text{Seed Rate (kg/ha)} = \dfrac{N \times W}{G \times P \times 100}}
$$

*where N = plants/ha, W = 1000-seed wt (g), G = germination fraction, P = purity fraction.*

**Alternative form (when G% and P% are in % form):**

$$
\boxed{\text{Seed Rate (kg/ha)} = \dfrac{N \times W \times 100}{G\% \times P\% \times 1000} = \dfrac{N \times W}{10 \times G\% \times P\%}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical

### 📝 Question
A farmer wants to grow **wheat** in 1 hectare. The desired plant population is **25,00,000 plants/ha** (25 lakh). The test weight is **40 g**. The seed lot has **90% germination** and **98% purity**. Calculate the seed rate in kg/ha.

### ✅ Solution

**Given:** N = 25,00,000 plants/ha, W = 40 g, G% = 90, P% = 98

**Step 1:** Write the formula.

$$
\text{Seed Rate} = \dfrac{N \times W}{10 \times G\% \times P\%}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$
\text{Seed Rate} = \dfrac{25{,}00{,}000 \times 40}{10 \times 90 \times 98}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute numerator.

$$
25{,}00{,}000 \times 40 = 10{,}00{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 4:** Compute denominator.

$$
10 \times 90 \times 98 = 10 \times 8820 = 88{,}200
$$

**Step 5:** Final division.

$$
\text{Seed Rate} = \dfrac{10{,}00{,}00{,}000}{88{,}200} = 113.37 \text{ kg/ha}
$$

**Step 6:** Interpretation.

> **A farmer needs approximately 113.4 kg of wheat seed per hectare** to achieve a plant population of 25 lakh/ha under the given seed quality conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer Validation Check:** Recommended KTU book seed rate for irrigated wheat is 100–125 kg/ha. Our answer (113.4 kg/ha) lies within this range ✅.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Method vs Crop

| Crop | Best Method | Reason | Alternate Method |
|------|-------------|--------|------------------|
| Rice (paddy) | **Transplanting** | Water control, weed suppression, higher yield | Direct seeding (D SR) |
| Wheat | **Drilling** | Uniform depth, light interception, saves seed | Broadcasting (small/marginal farmers) |
| Maize (hybrid) | **Dibbling** | Precise spacing, hybrid seed cost is high | Seed-cum-ferti drill |
| Cotton (hybrid) | **Dibbling** | Hybrid seed is expensive, precise placement saves seed | Planter with ridge-furrow |
| Groundnut | **Drilling** | Pod development needs loose soil | Dibbling (bold-seeded varieties) |
| Soybean | **Drilling** | Small-to-medium seed, row sowing facilitates aeration | Broadcasting (zero-till) |
| Sorghum | **Drilling** | Cereal, requires uniform depth | Dibbling (kharif sowing) |
| Pigeon pea | **Dibbling** | Bold seed, deep sowing, wide spacing | Drilling (large farms) |
| Mustard | **Broadcasting** | Very small seed, traditional practice | Drilling (line sowing) |
| Sugarcane | **Furrow planting (sett method)** | Vegetative propagation, no seed | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flow Diagram — Sowing Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Farmer decides to sow a crop] --> B{Available moisture condition?}
    B -- Optimum soil moisture --> C{High-value hybrid seed?}
    B -- Marginal or rain-fed --> D[Prefer Drilling or Broadcasting]
    B -- Submerged/puddled soil --> E[Transplanting]
    C -- Yes --> F[Dibbling]
    C -- No --> G[Drilling with seed-cum-ferti drill]
    D --> H{Soil type and seed size}
    H -- Small seed --> I[Broadcasting or seed drill with small hopper]
    H -- Bold seed --> J[Dibbling or planter]
    E --> K[Raise nursery for 3 to 6 weeks]
    K --> L[Transplant 25 to 30 day old seedlings]
    F --> M[Sow 1 to 2 seeds per hill at 60x30 cm spacing]
    G --> N[Drill at 20 to 45 cm row spacing]
    I --> O[Cover with light planking]
    J --> P[Cover with soil manually]
    L --> Q[Maintain 2 to 3 cm water depth]
    M --> R[Apply one life-saving irrigation within 3 days]
    N --> S[Light roller / planking for seed-soil contact]
    O --> T[Germination in 5 to 7 days]
    P --> T
    Q --> T
    R --> T
    S --> T
    T --> U[Thinning and gap filling at 15 to 20 DAS]
    U --> V[Established crop stand ready for intercultural operations]
```

## 4.2 Seed Rate Calculation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1: Identify Crop and Test Weight W] --> B[Step 2: Decide Desired Plant Population N per ha]
    B --> C[Step 3: Obtain Germination Percent G% from seed tag]
    C --> D[Step 4: Obtain Purity Percent P% from seed tag]
    D --> E[Step 5: Compute Pure Live Seed = G% times P% over 10000]
    E --> F[Step 6: Apply Formula SR equals N times W divided by 10 times G% times P%]
    F --> G[Step 7: Final Answer in kg per ha]
    G --> H[Validation: Cross-check with KTU standard table]
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Components of a Seed Drill

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        A1[Tractor PTO]

        A3[Ground wheel drive]
    end

        B1[Seed hopper with agitator]
        B2[Fluted roller metering device]
        B3[Inclined plate or cell meter]

        D2[Seed rate adjustment lever]
    end
    A1 --> B2
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B3
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    B1 --> D2
    B2 --> D2
    B3 --> D2
```
