## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Module 2 Model]**
**(a)** Describe in detail the different types of plant growing structures with neat diagrams, specifying their covering materials, structural features, and cost economics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polyhouse of floor area 200 m² has a single 200 µm LDPE cover with U = 6.5 W/m²·K and transmissivity 0.85. The cover area is 280 m². The internal volume is 600 m³, and the air change rate is 20 h⁻¹. Calculate the expected temperature rise inside the polyhouse at noon when the outside solar irradiance is 800 W/m². Assume the steady-state energy balance with conduction and ventilation losses. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Descriptive Answer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Detailed 7-mark answer framework — allocate marks as indicated.**

**(i) Greenhouse (2 marks)**
- Definition, structural components: gutter, ridge, purlin, column, glazing bars.
- Cladding: 4 mm float glass or 8–16 mm twin-wall polycarbonate.
- Frame: Hot-dip galvanized (HDG) steel or anodized aluminium.
- Cost: ₹3,000–6,000/m². Life: 15–25 years.
- Subsystems: pad-fan cooling, misting, CO₂ dosing, shading screens, heating.

**(ii) Polyhouse (2 marks)**
- Naturally ventilated structure, GI pipe frame (Class B), 200 µm UV-LDPE.
- Span: 3.2–8.0 m; gutter height: 2.5–4.0 m; side roll-up vents 1.5–2.0 m.
- Cost: ₹800–1,500/m². Life: 3–5 years for film; frame 15+ years.

**(iii) Net House (1.5 marks)**
- HDPE knitted shade net (50 %, 75 %, 90 %) or insect-proof net (40/50/200 mesh).
- Used for shade-loving crops and IPM; cost: ₹300–600/m²; life 5–8 years.

**(iv) Low & High Tunnels (1.5 marks)**
- Low tunnel: < 1 m height, GI wire/PVC hoops, PE film, used for nurseries.
- High tunnel: 3–5 m wide, walk-in, manual vent roll-up, used for strawberry/off-season vegetables.

*(Include a neat labelled cross-section diagram of a polyhouse.)*

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $A = 200 \, m^2$, $A_c = 280 \, m^2$, $V = 600 \, m^3$
- $U = 6.5 \, W/m^2 \cdot K$, $\tau = 0.85$
- $I = 800 \, W/m^2$, $ACH = 20 \, h^{-1}$
- $\rho_a = 1.2 \, kg/m^3$, $c_p = 1005 \, J/kg \cdot K$

**Step 1 — Solar heat gain inside the polyhouse:**

$$ Q_{in} = \tau \cdot I \cdot A $$

$$ Q_{in} = 0.85 \times 800 \times 200 = 136000 \, W $$

**Step 2 — Conduction loss coefficient:**

$$ H_{cond} = U \cdot A_c = 6.5 \times 280 = 1820 \, W/K $$

**Step 3 — Ventilation loss coefficient (convert ACH to per-second):**

$$ ACH_{s^{-1}} = \frac{20}{3600} = 5.556 \times 10^{-3} \, s^{-1} $$

$$ H_{vent} = \rho_a \cdot c_p \cdot V \cdot ACH_{s^{-1}} $$

$$ H_{vent} = 1.2 \times 1005 \times 600 \times 5.556 \times 10^{-3} $$

$$ H_{vent} = 4020 \, W/K $$

**Step 4 — Total heat loss coefficient:**

$$ H_{total} = H_{cond} + H_{vent} = 1820 + 4020 = 5840 \, W/K $$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\Delta T$ at steady state:**

$$ \Delta T = \frac{Q_{in}}{H_{total}} $$

$$ \Delta T = \frac{136000}{5840} \approx 23.3 \, ^\circ C $$

**Step 6 — Final answer:**

$$ \boxed{\Delta T \approx 23.3 \, ^\circ C \text{ above ambient} } $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** This is the theoretical maximum rise assuming no latent heat (ET) and no thermal storage. In a real tropical polyhouse, ET losses account for 30–50 % of the energy budget, so actual $\Delta T$ is typically 5–8 °C. The 23.3 °C result is **physically unrealistic** but mathematically correct under the stated assumptions — state this caveat explicitly in your exam answer.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 2 Model]**
**(a)** Compare shade net house and insect-proof net house with respect to design, mesh specifications, suitable crops, and role in IPM. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A grower in Thrissur wants to construct a polyhouse for capsicum cultivation. Recommend a suitable structure with justification based on Kerala's agro-climate, and estimate the capital cost for a 500 m² polyhouse at ₹1,200/m² including a fogger system (₹150/m² extra) and drip fertigation (₹80/m²). What is the cost per square metre of usable cultivation area? **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Comparison

| Parameter | Shade Net House | Insect-Proof Net House |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Reduce light intensity & temperature | Physically exclude insect pests |
| **Mesh Specification** | HDPE knitted net; shade % 50, 75, 90 (by yarn density) | Woven net; 40 mesh (945 µm aperture) for whiteflies; 50 mesh (355 µm) for thrips; 200 mesh for virus vectors |
| **Light Reduction** | By definition: 50/75/90 % reduction | 20–30 % (depending on mesh count) |
| **Suitable Crops** | Tea, coffee, pepper, cardamom, vanilla, orchids, nursery | Brinjal, okra, capsicum, tomato, cucumber (vegetables) |
| **Role in IPM** | Indirect (reduces heat stress, hence pest pressure) | Direct physical barrier; reduces pesticide need by up to 80 % |
| **Air-flow** | High (open weave) | Restricted → requires forced ventilation |
| **Cost** | ₹300–500/m² | ₹400–700/m² |
| **Frame** | GI pipe (Class B) or wooden poles | Same; often hybrid with polyhouse side walls |

#### Part (b) — Recommendation & Cost Estimation

**Recommendation:**
For capsicum (a cool-climate, high-value, pest-sensitive crop) in Thrissur's hot humid tropical climate, a **naturally ventilated polyhouse with pad-fan cooling and insect-proof side walls (50-mesh)** is recommended because:

1. Kerala's **monsoon humidity (RH 80–95 %)** causes severe fungal and viral diseases in capsicum — a covered structure with controlled ventilation is essential.
2. The **insect-proof net side walls** block whiteflies (the vector of *Pepper Mild Mottle Virus* and *Tomato Spotted Wilt Virus*), reducing pesticide spray cycles from 12 to 3 per cropping season.
3. **Pad-fan cooling** during March–May (when T_max > 35 °C) prevents flower drop and improves fruit set by 25–40 %.
4. NHM (National Horticulture Mission) provides a **50 % subsidy** on polyhouse construction in Kerala, making the LCC viable within 3–4 cropping cycles.

**Cost Calculation:**

| Component | Rate (₹/m²) | Area (m²) | Cost (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Polyhouse structure | 1,200 | 500 | 6,00,000 |
| Fogger system | 150 | 500 | 75,000 |
| Drip fertigation | 80 | 500 | 40,000 |
| **Total Capital Cost** | **1,430** | **500** | **7,15,000** |

$$ \text{Cost per m}^2 = \frac{715000}{500} = \boxed{₹1,430 / m^2} $$

**Cost per usable cultivation area** (assuming 90 % of floor area is plantable, 10 % lost to paths, headers, and equipment):

$$ A_{usable} = 500 \times 0.90 = 450 \, m^2 $$

$$ \text{Cost per m}^2_{\text{usable}} = \frac{715000}{450} \approx \boxed{₹1,588.9 / m^2} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ACH → s⁻¹ conversion** in ventilation problems. Marks lost: 2/7.
> 2. **Not stating assumptions** clearly in derivations (steady-state, no ET, etc.). Marks lost: 1/7.
> 3. **Confusing "shade net %" with "transmissivity"** — a 75 % shade net has $\tau = 0.25$, not 0.75. Marks lost: 1/3 in short answers.
> 4. **Writing glasshouse/polyhouse interchangeably** — they are **not** synonyms in KTU evaluation. Marks lost: 1/3.
> 5. **Omitting diagrams** in 7-mark descriptive questions. Marks lost: 2/7.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Types of Plant Growing Structures)**

- [x] **Five main structure types:** Greenhouse, Polyhouse, Net House, Low Tunnel, High Tunnel.
- [x] **Class A (Full CEA) = Glasshouse/Polyhouse with active climate control.**
- [x] **Class B (Semi-CEA) = Polyhouse with natural ventilation + optional fan-pad.**
- [x] **Class C (Protected only) = Net house, high tunnel — no active climate control.**
- [x] **Class D (Minimal protection) = Low tunnel, row cover.**
- [x] **Greenhouse effect equation:** $Q_{in} = Q_{cond} + Q_{vent} + Q_{ET} + Q_{stored}$.
- [x] **U-value of single glass = 5.8 W/m²·K; double PE ≈ 4.0 W/m²·K.**
- [x] **Transmissivity:** Glass 0.88–0.92; LDPE 0.80–0.90; 50 % shade net = 0.50.**
- [x] **Kerala-specific:** Polyhouse with insect-proof net + pad-fan is the gold standard for capsicum, gerbera, tomato.
- [x] **Cost hierarchy:** Low tunnel (₹50–100) < Net house (₹300–600) < High tunnel (₹400–700) < Polyhouse (₹800–1,500) < Glasshouse (₹3,000–6,000) per m².
- [x] **Always convert ACH from h⁻¹ to s⁻¹ (÷3600) in energy balance equations.**
- [x] **Lifecycle cost > Capital cost** when comparing structures.
- [x] **NHM subsidy available:** 50 % for polyhouse, 75 % for hi-tech greenhouse in Kerala.
- [x] **Key crops & structure mapping:**
  - Capsicum → Polyhouse with insect net
  - Cut flowers (rose, gerbera) → Polyhouse (naturally ventilated)
  - Tea / Coffee / Cardamom → 75 % shade net
  - Vegetable nursery → Low tunnel with non-woven fabric
  - Strawberry → High tunnel
  - Hydroponic lettuce → Pad-fan cooled greenhouse

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** For any 14-mark question on this topic, structure your answer in three parts: (1) Classification & definition, (2) Comparative table with at least 6 parameters, (3) Kerala-specific recommendation with cost. This framework scores full marks consistently in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pruning and Training
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT205 - CROP PRODUCTION & PROTECTION TECHNOLOGIES (AGRICULTURE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pruning and Training

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📚 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Pruning

**Pruning** is the horticultural practice of selectively removing specific parts of a plant—such as branches, buds, roots, or shoots—to improve the plant's health, structural integrity, fruiting/flowering capacity, and overall aesthetic or commercial value.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, pruning is classified as a **deliberate, regulated, and scheduled cultural intervention** that modifies the natural source-sink relationship of the plant, redistributes hormonal activity (particularly **auxins** and **cytokinins**), and alters the **canopy microclimate**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Ready Definition:**  
> *"Pruning is the science and art of removing plant parts (shoots, branches, roots, buds, or leaves) at the right time, in the right place, and to the right extent, with the objective of improving growth, productivity, quality, and longevity of the plant."*

## 1.2 Formal Academic Definition of Training

**Training** is the horticultural practice of physically guiding and shaping a plant's growth on a **support structure** (trellis, stake, pergola, or wall) during its early developmental stages, so that it acquires a **predetermined architecture** suitable for maximum light interception, ease of management, and mechanical harvesting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (High-Yield for KTU):**  
> - **Pruning = Subtractive** (removing existing parts)  
> - **Training = Additive/Directive** (adding support and guiding new growth)  
> Both practices are **complementary** and almost always applied together in commercial orchards and vineyards.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

### 🧠 The "Salon and School" Analogy

Imagine a young child:

- **Pruning** is like a **haircut at a salon** ✂️ — You selectively cut off the messy, overgrown, dead, or unwanted parts so that the remaining parts look healthy, grow better, and the overall shape becomes attractive. A haircut doesn't change *who* the person is, but it makes them look better and feel lighter.

- **Training** is like **sending the child to school** 🏫 — You teach them discipline, posture, and structure from an early age. You might use a stick (props/stakes), define boundaries (walls/trellises), and teach them manners (heading back, pinching) so they grow into a well-shaped adult.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Example:**  
> In a **grape vineyard**, vines are *trained* on a **Y-trellis or kniffin system** (school), and every winter they are *pruned* back to a fixed number of buds (salon). The two practices work hand-in-hand.

## 1.4 Why Are Pruning and Training Performed? (KTU-Listed Objectives)

- To **maintain the desired shape and size** of the plant
- To **increase yield and quality** of fruits/flowers
- To **balance vegetative and reproductive growth**
- To **improve light penetration** and air circulation within the canopy
- To **remove diseased, dead, or damaged** wood (sanitation)
- To **facilitate cultural operations** like spraying, harvesting, and intercultural activities
- To **regulate the bearing cycle** (e.g., overcoming alternate bearing in mango)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Students often confuse the *order* of operations. Always remember: **Training begins FIRST** (during the formative years), and **pruning follows** throughout the plant's life.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principles of Pruning (The 4 D's)

Every pruning cut must satisfy these four biological principles:

1. **De-suckering** — Removal of water sprouts and root suckers
2. **De-branching** — Removal of unwanted, crossing, or inward-growing branches
3. **De-flowering / De-fruiting** — Selective removal to balance crop load
4. **De-topping / Heading back** — Cutting back terminal growth

## 2.2 Types of Pruning — Complete Classification

### A. Based on Plant Part Removed

| **Type** | **Plant Part Removed** | **Example Crops** | **Season** |
|----------|------------------------|-------------------|------------|
| **Root Pruning** | Portion of roots | Transplanting seedlings, bonsai | Dormant season |
| **Shoot/Stem Pruning** | Shoots/stems | Tea, coffee, mulberry | Year-round (selective) |
| **Leaf Pruning** | Leaves | Lettuce, cabbage blanching | Pre-harvest |
| **Bud Pruning (Disbudding)** | Flower/fruit buds | Grapes, apple, mango | Pre-bloom |
| **Fruit Pruning (Thinning)** | Fruits | Mango, apple, peach | Post fruit-set |

### B. Based on Timing (The Most Frequently Asked KTU Question!)

| **Timing** | **Name** | **Period** | **Purpose** |
|-----------|----------|------------|-------------|
| **Winter / Dormant** | **Dry Pruning** (Pre-bloom) | Dec–Feb in India | Shape + stimulate vigorous growth |
| **Summer / Growing** | **Green Pruning** (Mid-season) | April–June | Remove water sprouts, improve light |
| **Post-harvest** | **Carry-over Pruning** | Immediately after harvest | Prepare for next season's flush |
| **Pre-flowering** | **Desuckering/Thinning** | Just before bloom | Balance bloom load |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "DGS-P":**  
> **D**ormant, **G**reen, **S**ummer-thinning, **P**ost-harvest — the 4 standard pruning timings.

### C. Based on Method of Cutting

- **Thinning Out** — Complete removal of a shoot/branch at its point of origin
- **Heading Back** — Cutting back a shoot to a lateral bud (stimulates branching)
- **Pinching** — Removing the soft growing tip with fingers (used in chrysanthemum, tomato seedlings)
- **Disbudding** — Removal of unwanted flower/fruit buds
- **De-suckering** — Removal of water sprouts from main branches or trunk
- **Pollarding** — Severe heading back of all branches (used in shade trees, mulberry)
- **Coppicing** — Cutting the entire plant near ground level (used in eucalyptus, bamboo)
- **Pollarding** vs **Coppicing** distinction is **HIGH-YIELD** for KTU!

> [!WARNING]
> **Board Pitfall:** Pollarding = cut at 1–2 m height (tree retains trunk).  
> Coppicing = cut at ground level (entire top removed). Many students write them as same.

## 2.3 Types of Training Systems

| **Training System** | **Description** | **Used In** |
|--------------------|------------------|-------------|
| **Bush System** | Plant trained as a low bush with 3–5 main branches | Guava, pomegranate, papaya |
| **Single Stem System** | Single vertical stem, all laterals removed up to a height | Coconut, oil palm |
| **Crown System** | Single stem with a defined canopy at top | Mango (initial years) |
| **Espalier System** | Plant trained flat against a wall/trellis in geometric patterns | Apple, pear, ornamentals |
| **Trellis System (Kniffin)** | Vine trained on horizontal wires with arms | Grape (girdle varieties) |
| **Y-Trellis / GDC** | Two inclined arms forming a 'Y' shape | Grape (Vitis vinifera) |
| **Pergola / Bower** | Horizontal overhead canopy system | Grape (Bangalore Blue), passion fruit |
| **Head System** | Trained with a clear trunk up to 1 m and a compact head | Apple, pear (on spur types) |
| **Modified Leader / Cordon** | Central leader with horizontal cordons | Apple (MM rootstocks) |

## 2.4 Tools and Equipment Used in Pruning

- **Secateurs (Hand Pruners)** — For shoots < 2.5 cm diameter
- **Loppers** — For branches 2.5–5 cm diameter
- **Pruning Saw** — For branches > 5 cm
- **Hedge Shears** — For hedge plants
- **Pole Pruners** — For tall trees
- **Disinfectant** — Bleach/copper-based, to sterilize tools between plants

> [!TIP]
> **Hygiene Rule:** Always disinfect tools after pruning a diseased plant to prevent pathogen transmission. This is a **frequently asked viva question**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Metrics Cheat Sheet

Since pruning/training is more skill-based than calculation-based, the key "formulas" are actually **practical ratios, counts, and time-metrics**.

| **Metric / Ratio** | **Symbol/Formula** | **Typical Value** | **Used For** |
|--------------------|--------------------|--------------------|---------------|
| **Pruning Intensity** | $PI = \dfrac{\text{Number of buds left}}{\text{Number of buds removed}} \times 100$ | 40–60% for grape | Quantifying pruning severity |
| **Bud Load (Grape)** | $BL = \text{Buds per vine}$ | 40–80 buds/vine (varies with variety) | Yield estimation |
| **Spacing Standard (Grape)** | $R$ (row) × $P$ (plant) | $3.0 \text{ m} \times 1.5 \text{ m}$ (bower) | Training system design |
| **Trellis Height (Standard)** | $H_{trellis}$ | 1.8 – 2.0 m | Y-trellis, kniffin |
| **Training Period (Years)** | $T_{train}$ | 3–4 years (fruit trees) | Establishment phase |
| **Pruning Wound Paste** | Bordeaux / Copper paste | — | Disease prevention |
| **Ideal Pruning Cut Angle** | $\theta$ | $45°$ from branch | Water runoff, healing |
| **Sap Flow Rate (general)** | $Q = A \times v$ | — | Conceptual; high sap flow periods avoid heavy pruning |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pruning Cut:** Always make a clean cut at a **45° angle**, slanting away from the bud, so that water does not accumulate on the cut surface and cause rotting.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **precision agriculture**, modern orchards use:
- **Mechanical pruning machines** (disc-type and cutter-bar) for uniform hedge-row systems
- **LiDAR-based canopy mapping** to compute optimal pruning volumes
- **Drone-based NDVI imaging** to identify dead wood for targeted removal
- **Robotic pruners** being developed in apple and grape vineyards globally

This makes pruning & training a **direct application of agricultural engineering** in crop production technology.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔍 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedures & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Pruning Procedure (KTU Lab-Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following 10-step procedure is the **KTU 2024 Scheme recommended protocol** for pruning any woody perennial.

### Step 1: Inspection and Diagnosis
Identify the **type of pruning required** by examining the plant. Look for:
- Dead, diseased, or damaged (3D) wood
- Crossing or rubbing branches
- Water sprouts and root suckers
- Crowded canopy with poor light penetration

### Step 2: Sanitize Tools
Disinfect secateurs/loppers with **70% ethanol** or **1% sodium hypochlorite** solution. This step is mandatory to prevent cross-infection.

### Step 3: Remove the 3D Wood First
Cut out all **Dead, Diseased, and Damaged** branches at their point of origin.

### Step 4: Remove Suckers and Water Sprouts
Eliminate vigorous vertical shoots that arise from the rootstock (suckers) or main trunk (water sprouts). These consume nutrients but rarely bear fruit.

### Step 5: Open Up the Canopy
Remove **crossing, inward-growing, and parallel branches** to improve air circulation and light penetration.

### Step 6: Apply Thinning Cuts
Use **thinning cuts** to remove entire branches/shoots back to the parent branch. This opens the canopy without stimulating excessive new growth.

### Step 7: Apply Heading Back Cuts
Use **heading back cuts** on selected shoots to stimulate lateral branching, especially in young plants being shaped.

### Step 8: Apply Wound Dressing
For cuts > 2.5 cm diameter, apply **Bordeaux paste** or a commercial wound sealant to prevent fungal/bacterial entry.

### Step 9: Dispose of Pruned Material
Burn or compost the pruned material. **Do NOT leave it near the orchard** as it may harbour pathogens.

### Step 10: Record and Document
Maintain a **pruning register** recording:
- Date of pruning
- Number of cuts
- Estimated canopy volume removed
- Variety and age of plant

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Training Procedure (Example: Grape on Y-Trellis)

### Mathematical Layout of a Y-Trellis

The Y-trellis has two inclined arms. The angle between the arms is typically **100° to 120°**, and the trellis height is **1.8 to 2.0 m**.

The horizontal spread of each arm can be calculated as:

$$W_{arm} = \frac{H_{trellis}}{\tan(\theta/2)}$$

Where:
- $W_{arm}$ = horizontal spread of one arm (m)
- $H_{trellis}$ = vertical height of trellis (m)
- $\theta$ = total angle between the two inclined arms

**Worked Example:**

Given: $H_{trellis} = 2.0$ m, $\theta = 120°$

$$W_{arm} = \frac{2.0}{\tan(60°)} = \frac{2.0}{1.732} \approx 1.155 \text{ m}$$

Therefore, the **total canopy width** at the top = $2 \times W_{arm} = 2.31$ m.

This calculation helps engineers design the **row-to-row spacing** of the vineyard to avoid canopy overlap.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Prune vs Train

| **Scenario** | **Action** | **Tool/Method** | **Season** | **Expected Outcome** |
|--------------|-----------|------------------|------------|----------------------|
| Young plant, 0–3 years | **Train only** | Stake, tie, heading back | Year-round | Strong framework |
| Mature plant, dormant | **Prune + Train** | Secateurs, loppers | Winter (Dec–Feb) | Shape + yield |
| Diseased plant | **Sanitation pruning** | Disinfected tools | As noticed | Stop disease spread |
| Vigorous vegetative growth | **Heavy heading back** | Loppers, saw | Post-harvest | Balance vigour |
| Poor light penetration | **Thinning cuts** | Secateurs | Summer | Better fruit colour |
| Alternate bearing (mango) | **Annual pruning** | Whole-tree renewal | After harvest | Regular bearing |
| Hedge-row orchard (apple) | **Mechanical pruning** | Tractor-mounted cutter | Dormant | Uniform hedgerow |

## 3.4 Pruning Intensity Calculation — Detailed Derivation

Let us derive the **Pruning Intensity (PI)** in a grape vine.

**Given data:**
- Total buds present before pruning, $B_{total} = 500$ buds
- Number of buds retained after pruning, $B_{retained} = 200$ buds

**Step 1:** Calculate the number of buds removed.

$$B_{removed} = B_{total} - B_{retained} = 500 - 200 = 300 \text{ buds}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the PI formula.

$$PI = \frac{B_{retained}}{B_{removed}} \times 100$$

$$PI = \frac{200}{300} \times 100 = 66.67\%$$

**Step 3:** Interpret the result.

$$PI > 60\% \Rightarrow \text{Light Pruning (high bud retention)}$$
$$PI < 30\% \Rightarrow \text{Heavy Pruning (low bud retention)}$$

**Step 4:** Recommend the action.

In our example, $PI = 66.67\%$, which is **light pruning** — suitable for vigorous vines in fertile soils. For weak vines in poor soils, a PI of 30–40% (heavier pruning) is recommended.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb:** Vigorous varieties + fertile soil = Light Pruning. Weak varieties + poor soil = Heavy Pruning.

## 3.5 Trellis Cost Estimation (Optional Engineering Application)

Given a vineyard of area $A$ = 1 hectare = **10,000 m²**

**Row spacing** = 3.0 m, **Plant spacing** = 1.5 m

**Number of rows:**

$$N_{rows} = \frac{L_{field}}{R_{spacing}}$$

Assuming a square field of side $\sqrt{10000} = 100$ m:

$$N_{rows} = \frac{100}{3.0} = 33 \text{ rows}$$

**Number of plants per row:**

$$N_{plants/row} = \frac{100}{1.5} = 66 \text{ plants}$$

**Total plants:**

$$N_{total} = 33 \times 66 = 2178 \text{ plants}$$

**Trellis posts required** (1 post per 6 m along each row):

$$N_{posts/row} = \frac{100}{6} = 17 \text{ posts}$$

$$N_{posts,total} = 33 \times 17 = 561 \text{ posts}$$

This kind of **engineering calculation** is often asked in KTU's application-oriented questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Pruning Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Inspect Plant] --> B{Is Plant 0-3 Years Old?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Apply TRAINING Only: Stake, Tie, Shape]
    B -- No --> D{Is Plant Dormant?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Apply DORMANT PRUNING: Shape + Framework]
    D -- No --> F{Is It Growing Season?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Apply GREEN PRUNING: De-sucker, De-branch]
    F -- No --> H[Apply POST-HARVEST PRUNING: Renewal]
    E --> I[Apply Bordeaux Paste on Cuts > 2.5 cm]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    C --> J[Record Pruning Details in Register]
    I --> J
    J --> K[End]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Pruning & Training System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT STAGE]
        A1[Plant Material: Variety, Age, Rootstock]
        A2[Environmental Data: Climate, Soil, Spacing]
```
