Cultivation Practices of Horticultural Crops: Vegetable Crops, Fruit Crops, Flower Crops. Basic Principles of Natural Farming, Organic Farming and Sustainable Agriculture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Liquid Fertilizers — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Liquid Fertilizer (KTU Definition):** A liquid fertilizer is a nutrient solution in which the essential plant nutrients (N, P, K and secondary/micro-nutrients) are either completely dissolved in water (clear liquid fertilizers) or held in suspension (suspension fertilizers), applied through soil drench, foliar spray, fertigation, or foliar nutrition systems.

The **key metrics** of liquid fertilizers are:

- **Specific Gravity:** Typically **1.20 – 1.35 g/cm³** at 25 °C
- **Salt-out Temperature:** The temperature at which dissolved salts crystallize out of solution (lower = better)
- **pH Range:** **5.5 – 7.0** is optimal to avoid leaf burn and ensure stability
- **Concentration:** Expressed as percentage by weight (w/w) or weight/volume (w/v)

**Major Categories of Liquid Fertilizers:**

| Category | Examples | N-P-K Range |
|----------|----------|-------------|
| **Clear Liquid Fertilizers (CLF)** | Urea Ammonium Nitrate (UAN), Ammonium Polyphosphate (APP) | 10-34-0, 32-0-0, 11-37-0 |
| **Suspension Fertilizers** | Clay-based suspensions of K₂SO₄, micronutrients | 9-27-0, 13-13-13 |
| **Solution Fertilizers** | NPK solutions with all nutrients in true solution | 8-8-8, 4-10-10 |
| **Foliar Fertilizers** | Multi-K, KNO₃, MgSO₄ sprays | Variable, low salt index |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Intuition:** Think of a liquid fertilizer like a "ready-to-drink health smoothie for plants." Just as a smoothie has all the vitamins dissolved in liquid form so your body absorbs them instantly, a liquid fertilizer has nutrients already dissolved so plant roots and leaves absorb them much faster than granular fertilizers (which must first dissolve in soil moisture before uptake).

## 1.2 Solubility — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solubility:** The maximum amount of a fertilizer salt (in grams) that can dissolve in 100 grams of water at a specified temperature to form a saturated solution. For KTU agronomy, this is critical because two fertilizers in a tank mix must remain in solution without precipitating out.

**Solubility Equation (General):**

$$S = \frac{m_{solute}}{m_{solvent}} \times 100 \quad \text{(g/100g water)}$$

Where $S$ is solubility, $m_{solute}$ is mass of dissolved fertilizer, and $m_{solvent}$ is mass of water.

## 1.3 Compatibility — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fertilizer Compatibility:** The ability of two or more fertilizer salts to be mixed in a concentrated tank solution without undergoing chemical reactions that produce insoluble precipitates, reduce nutrient availability, or cause phytotoxicity.

There are two kinds:
- **Chemical Compatibility:** No precipitation reactions (e.g., mixing calcium nitrate with sulphate forms insoluble CaSO₄)
- **Physical Compatibility:** No phase separation, settling, or viscosity changes in the mix.

## 1.4 Plant Pathology & Pest Management — Core Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plant Disease:** Any physiological or morphological deviation from the normal functioning of a plant, caused by a living organism (pathogen) or by abiotic stress.

**The Disease Triangle (Fundamental Concept):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Disease Triangle:** A plant disease is like a "fire triangle." For a fire, you need fuel + oxygen + heat. For a plant disease, you need a **susceptible host** + **virulent pathogen** + **favourable environment**. Remove any one vertex, and the "fire" (disease) cannot start. This is why IPM works by attacking one of these three corners.

**Key Terminology:**

- **Pathogen:** Microorganism causing disease (fungi, bacteria, viruses, nematodes, mycoplasmas)
- **Symptom:** Visible expression of disease in the plant (e.g., leaf spot, blight, wilt)
- **Sign:** Actual physical presence of the pathogen (e.g., mycelium, spores)
- **Pest:** Any organism (insect, mite, rodent, bird, weed) that competes with or damages crops
- **Economic Threshold Level (ETL):** The pest population density at which control measures should be applied to prevent the pest population from reaching the **Economic Injury Level (EIL)**

## 1.5 Integrated Methods — Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Integrated Nutrient Management (INM):** A holistic approach combining organic manures, biofertilizers, green manures, crop residues, and chemical fertilizers in a balanced proportion to sustain soil fertility and crop productivity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Integrated Pest Management (IPM):** An ecologically-based pest control strategy that uses all suitable methods in a compatible manner to maintain pest populations below ETL, minimizing economic, health, and environmental risks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Integrated Water Management (IWM):** Coordinated management of water resources including micro-irrigation (drip & sprinkler), mulching, rainwater harvesting, and deficit irrigation strategies to optimize water-use efficiency (WUE).

## 1.6 Field Crops of Kerala — Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Field Crops:** Crops cultivated on a large area in open fields, primarily for food, fibre, oil, and commercial uses. In Kerala's laterite and alluvial soils, the major field crops include rice (cereal), pulses (grain legumes), coconut-based cropping systems, sugarcane, and fibre crops.

**Cereals in Kerala Context:** Rice (the staple), Maize
**Grain Legumes:** Cowpea, Green gram, Black gram, Red gram, Horse gram
**Oilseed Crops:** Groundnut, Coconut, Sesamum
**Sugar Crops:** Sugarcane
**Fibre Crops:** Cotton, Jute, Mesta (Kenaf)

## 1.7 Horticultural Crops — Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Horticulture:** The branch of agriculture dealing with the cultivation of fruits, vegetables, flowers, and ornamental plants. It encompasses three sub-branches:
> - **Olericulture:** Vegetable cultivation
> - **Pomology:** Fruit cultivation
> - **Floriculture:** Flower cultivation

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Horticultural Trinity:** Think of horticulture as the "three sisters of intensive farming" — Vegetables (olericulture) feed the body, Fruits (pomology) feed the soul with sweetness, and Flowers (floriculture) feed the spirit with beauty. Together they form Kerala's high-value crop portfolio.

## 1.8 Natural, Organic, and Sustainable Agriculture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Organic Farming:** A system of farming that prohibits the use of synthetic agrochemicals (fertilizers, pesticides, herbicides, growth regulators) and relies on crop rotations, organic manures, biofertilizers, biopesticides, and biological control for sustainable production.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Natural Farming (Subhash Palekar's Zero Budget Natural Farming - ZBNF):** A chemical-free farming system that uses on-farm inputs like Jeevamrutha, Beejamrutha, Mulching, and Whapasa (soil moisture).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sustainable Agriculture:** A balanced approach that integrates productivity with environmental stewardship, economic viability, and social equity to meet current needs without compromising the ability of future generations to meet theirs.

**The Three Pillars of Sustainable Agriculture:**

1. **Environmental Sustainability** (soil, water, biodiversity)
2. **Economic Sustainability** (profitability, livelihood)
3. **Social Sustainability** (equity, food security)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theory of Liquid Fertilizer Solubility

### 2.1.1 Solubility Curves and Temperature Dependence

Most fertilizer salts show **positive temperature coefficients of solubility** — they dissolve more as temperature increases. The solubility of a salt in water is governed by the **van't Hoff equation**:

$$\ln S = -\frac{\Delta H_{sol}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T}\right) + C$$

Where:
- $S$ = solubility (mol/L)
- $\Delta H_{sol}$ = enthalpy of solution (J/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $C$ = integration constant

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters:** When two fertilizers with different $\Delta H_{sol}$ values are mixed, the combined solution may have a salt-out temperature higher than either individual solution. This is the fundamental reason why not all fertilizer combinations are storage-stable in cold weather.

### 2.1.2 Salt Index Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Salt Index (SI):** A measure of the osmotic effect a fertilizer exerts on soil water relative to sodium nitrate (NaNO₃ = 100). Lower salt index = less risk of root burn.

$$\text{Salt Index} = \frac{\Delta \psi_{fertilizer}}{\Delta \psi_{NaNO_3}} \times 100$$

Where $\Delta \psi$ is the change in osmotic potential of the soil solution.

| Fertilizer | Salt Index | N-P-K |
|-----------|-----------|-------|
| Urea | 75.4 | 46-0-0 |
| Ammonium Nitrate | 104.7 | 34-0-0 |
| Potassium Chloride (MOP) | 116.3 | 0-0-60 |
| Potassium Nitrate | 73.6 | 13-0-38 |
| Ammonium Sulphate | 88.3 | 21-0-0 |

### 2.1.3 Common Ion Effect (Critical for Compatibility)

When two salts share a common ion, solubility of one is suppressed:

$$Ca^{2+}(aq) + SO_4^{2-}(aq) \rightleftharpoons CaSO_4(s) \downarrow$$

The **Ksp (solubility product)** controls precipitation:

$$K_{sp} = [Ca^{2+}][SO_4^{2-}]$$

If the **ion product** $[Ca^{2+}][SO_4^{2-}] > K_{sp}$, precipitation occurs.

> [!WARNING]
> **Compatibility Rule #1:** Never mix **Calcium-bearing fertilizers** (Ca(NO₃)₂) with **Sulphate-bearing fertilizers** (K₂SO₄, (NH₄)₂SO₄) in a concentrated tank mix. CaSO₄ (Gypsum) precipitates and clogs nozzles and drip lines.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Liquid Fertilizers & Agronomy

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Definition | Units | Application |
|---|-------------------|----------------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Solubility (g/100g) | $S = \frac{m_{solute}}{m_{solvent}} \times 100$ | g/100g | Determining if salts stay in solution |
| 2 | Mass-Mass % | $\%w/w = \frac{m_{solute}}{m_{solution}} \times 100$ | % | Concentration of liquid fertilizer |
| 3 | Mass-Volume % | $\%w/v = \frac{m_{solute}(g)}{V_{solution}(mL)} \times 100$ | % | Foliar spray concentration |
| 4 | Solubility Product | $K_{sp} = \prod [ions]^n$ | varies | Precipitation check |
| 5 | Salt Index | $SI = \frac{\Delta \psi_f}{\Delta \psi_{NaNO_3}} \times 100$ | – | Phytotoxicity risk |
| 6 | Fertilizer Rate | $R = \frac{N_{req}}{\%N/100}$ | kg/ha | Calculate dose |
| 7 | EIL (Economic Injury Level) | $EIL = \frac{C}{V \cdot D \cdot K}$ | insects/unit | Pest management |
| 8 | ETL (Economic Threshold) | $ETL \approx 0.5 \times EIL$ | insects/unit | When to spray |
| 9 | Field Capacity (Vol.) | $FC\% = \frac{V_{water}}{V_{soil}} \times 100$ | % | Irrigation scheduling |
| 10 | Water Use Efficiency | $WUE = \frac{Y}{ET_c}$ | kg/m³ | Drip irrigation design |
| 11 | Seed Rate (kg/ha) | $SR = \frac{TGW \times 10^6}{Germ\% \times 10^4 \times 100}$ | kg/ha | Crop sowing |
| 12 | Yield (q/ha) | $Y = \frac{Total\ yield\ (kg)}{Area\ (ha)} \times \frac{1}{100}$ | q/ha | Production estimate |
| 13 | Plant Population | $PP = \frac{Area\ (m^2)}{Row\ spacing \times Plant\ spacing}$ | plants/m² | Sowing geometry |
| 14 | B:C Ratio | $BCR = \frac{Gross\ Return}{Total\ Cost}$ | ratio | Economic analysis |
| 15 | Organic Carbon (Walkley-Black) | $OC\% = \frac{(B-S) \times N \times 0.003 \times 100}{W}$ | % | Soil fertility |

**Notation:** $C$ = control cost, $V$ = market value, $D$ = damage per insect, $K$ = proportionate reduction, $Y$ = yield, $ET_c$ = crop evapotranspiration, $TGW$ = thousand grain weight.

## 2.3 Theory of Pest & Disease Management

### 2.3.1 The Disease Triangle — Detailed Analysis

$$D_{severity} = f(H, P, E) \quad \text{where } D = 0 \text{ if any of } H,P,E = 0$$

Where:
- $H$ = Host susceptibility (0 to 1)
- $P$ = Pathogen virulence/inoculum density
- $E$ = Environmental favourability (humidity, temperature, leaf wetness)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why IPM Works Mathematically:** IPM aims to set at least one of H, P, or E toward zero. Resistant varieties reduce H; biocontrol reduces P; cultural practices (drainage, spacing) reduce E. In mathematical terms, IPM is a **multiplicative system** — push any factor toward zero, and disease severity collapses.

### 2.3.2 Economic Injury Level — Stern's Equation

$$EIL = \frac{C}{V \times D \times K}$$

- $C$ = Cost of control (₹/ha)
- $V$ = Market value of crop (₹/quintal)
- $D$ = Yield loss per pest unit (q/ha per insect)
- $K$ = Proportionate reduction in pest population due to control

The **Economic Threshold Level (ETL)** is typically set lower than EIL to allow time for control measures to act:

$$ETL \approx 0.5 \times EIL \text{ to } 0.75 \times EIL$$

### 2.3.3 ETL for Common Pests (Kerala Crops)

| Pest | Crop | ETL (per plant/leaf) |
|------|------|----------------------|
| Rice Stem Borer (Dead heart) | Rice | 5% dead hearts or 1 moth/trap |
| Brown Plant Hopper | Rice | 5-10 insects/hill |
| Leaf Folder | Rice | 10% damaged leaves |
| Fruit Fly | Mango | 5 flies/trap/week |
| Red Palm Weevil | Coconut | 1 weevil/10 palms |
| Rhinoceros Beetle | Coconut | 1 beetle/3 palms |
| Tea Mosquito Bug | Cashew | 25% infested shoots |

## 2.4 Theory of IPM and INM

### 2.4.1 IPM Decision Hierarchy

1. **Cultural Control** (rotation, sowing date, plant density)
2. **Mechanical Control** (traps, handpicking, sticky boards)
3. **Biological Control** (parasitoids, predators, biopesticides)
4. **Botanical/Neem-based** (azadirachtin)
5. **Chemical Control** (last resort, with resistance management)

### 2.4.2 INM — Quantitative Nutrient Equation

$$N_{total} = N_{fertilizer} + N_{organic} + N_{biofix} + N_{residual}$$

Typical INM ratio for Kerala rice:
- **Chemical fertilizer:** 50% of total N
- **FYM / Compost:** 25% of total N
- **Green manure (Sesbania):** 15% of total N
- **Biofertilizer (Azolla / BGA / Azospirillum):** 10% of total N

## 2.5 Theory of Crop Production — Field Crops

### 2.5.1 Growth Stages of Rice (Critical for KTU)

| Stage | DAS | Description | Management |
|-------|-----|-------------|------------|
| Seedling | 0-15 | 2-3 leaf stage | Water 2-3 cm, gap filling |
| Tillering | 15-45 | Active tillering | N top-dressing, weed control |
| Panicle Initiation | 45-60 | Floret differentiation | Critical irrigation, K application |
| Booting | 60-75 | Swollen sheath | Pest surveillance |
| Flowering | 75-90 | Anthesis | Avoid water stress |
| Maturity | 90-130 | Grain filling → harvest | Drain field 1 week before harvest |

### 2.5.2 Cereal Crop Comparisons (Kerala Adapted)

| Crop | Season | Seed Rate | Spacing | Fertilizer NPK (kg/ha) | Yield (q/ha) |
|------|--------|-----------|---------|------------------------|--------------|
| Rice (Pokkali) | Jun-Oct | 60-75 kg/ha | 15×10 cm | 70-35-35 | 20-25 |
| Rice (Wayanad) | Sept-Dec | 60-75 kg/ha | 15×10 cm | 90-45-45 | 45-55 |
| Maize | Jun-Jul, Sep-Oct | 18-20 kg/ha | 60×20 cm | 135-65-65 | 30-40 |
| Ragi | Jun-Jul | 5 kg/ha | 25×10 cm | 50-25-25 | 12-15 |

### 2.5.3 Grain Legumes — Key Features

| Crop | N-Fixation | Rhizobium | Seed Rate | Spacing |
|------|-----------|-----------|-----------|---------|
| Cowpea | 50-100 kg/ha | *R. leguminosarum* | 20-25 kg/ha | 30×15 cm |
| Green gram | 30-50 kg/ha | *R. phaseoli* | 20 kg/ha | 25×10 cm |
| Black gram | 30-50 kg/ha | *R. phaseoli* | 20 kg/ha | 25×10 cm |
| Red gram | 100-200 kg/ha | *R. cajani* | 15 kg/ha | 60×20 cm |
| Horse gram | 30-40 kg/ha | Cowpea group | 25 kg/ha | 30×10 cm |

## 2.6 Horticultural Crops — Production Principles

### 2.6.1 Vegetable Crop Categories

| Type | Examples | KTU Relevance |
|------|----------|---------------|
| **Solanaceous** | Tomato, Brinjal, Chilli, Capsicum | Major Kerala vegetables |
| **Cucurbits** | Cucumber, Bitter gourd, Snake gourd, Pumpkin | Homestead cultivation |
| **Legume vegetables** | Cowpea, Yard-long bean | Dual purpose |
| **Root & Tuber** | Carrot, Beetroot, Radish, Tapioca | Storage, value addition |
| **Leafy** | Amaranthus, Spinach, Cabbage | Kitchen gardens |

### 2.6.2 Fruit Crops of Kerala

| Crop | Botanical Name | Propagation | Spacing | Bearing Age |
|------|---------------|-------------|---------|-------------|
| Coconut | *Cocos nucifera* | Seedling | 7.5×7.5 m | 6-8 yrs |
| Banana | *Musa paradisiaca* | Suckers | 2×2 m | 12-15 months |
| Mango | *Mangifera indica* | Grafting | 10×10 m | 4-6 yrs |
| Jackfruit | *Artocarpus heterophyllus* | Seedling | 10×10 m | 5-7 yrs |
| Cashew | *Anacardium occidentale* | Seed/Graft | 7×7 m | 3-4 yrs |
| Papaya | *Carica papaya* | Seed | 2×2 m | 8-10 months |
| Pineapple | *Ananas comosus* | Suckers | 60×30 cm | 18-24 months |

### 2.6.3 Flower Crops of Kerala

| Crop | Use | Key Feature |
|------|-----|-------------|
| Jasmine (Malli) | Loose flower, garlands | Year-round flowering |
| Rose | Cut flower, garden | Requires polyhouse in heavy rain |
| Tuberose | Cut flower, perfume | Long spike, fragrant |
| Marigold | Garland, religious | Easy to grow, pest-resistant |
| Crossandra | Loose flower | Profitable in Thiruvananthapuram |
| Anthurium | Cut flower (export) | Needs polyhouse, 70% shade |

## 2.7 Natural / Organic / Sustainable Agriculture — Theoretical Pillars

### 2.7.1 Four Pillars of ZBNF (Palekar's Natural Farming)

1. **Bijamrita** — Seed treatment with cow-dung, cow urine, lime, soil
2. **Jeevamrita** — Soil microbial culture (cow dung + cow urine + jaggery + pulse flour + soil)
3. **Mulch (Achchadana)** — Soil + straw mulch to conserve moisture
4. **Whapasa** — Soil having both air + moisture molecules (no continuous flooding)

### 2.7.2 Organic Certification in India

- **NPOP** (National Programme for Organic Production) — governs standards
- **Participatory Guarantee System (PGS-India)** — for small farmers
- **Conversion period:** 2-3 years for annuals, 3 years for perennials
- **Buffer zone:** 5-10 m between organic and conventional fields

### 2.7.3 Sustainable Agriculture Indicators

- **Soil Organic Carbon (SOC):** target >0.8% in tropical soils
- **Water Use Efficiency (WUE):** target >1.5 kg/m³ for rice
- **Biodiversity Index (Shannon):** target >2.0
- **Net Energy Ratio (NER):** target >3
- **Greenhouse Gas (GHG) Emission:** target <2.5 t CO₂-eq/ha/yr

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Solubility of a Mixed Fertilizer Solution

**Problem:** A farmer wants to mix 25 kg of KNO₃ (solubility = 32 g/100g water at 25°C) and 15 kg of Urea (solubility = 108 g/100g water at 25°C) in 100 L of water. Check if both remain fully dissolved.

**Step 1: Convert water volume to mass**
Given water density ≈ 1 kg/L, so 100 L = 100 kg water.

**Step 2: Calculate concentration of KNO₃ in solution**

$$S_{KNO_3}^{actual} = \frac{25 \text{ kg}}{100 \text{ kg water}} \times 100 = 25 \text{ g/100g water}$$

**Step 3: Compare with maximum solubility**

$$S_{KNO_3}^{actual} = 25 \text{ g/100g} < S_{KNO_3}^{max} = 32 \text{ g/100g}$$

Since 25 < 32, KNO₃ stays fully dissolved. ✓

**Step 4: Calculate concentration of Urea**

$$S_{urea}^{actual} = \frac{15 \text{ kg}}{100 \text{ kg water}} \times 100 = 15 \text{ g/100g water}$$

**Step 5: Compare with urea solubility**

$$S_{urea}^{actual} = 15 \text{ g/100g} < S_{urea}^{max} = 108 \text{ g/100g}$$

Urea stays fully dissolved. ✓

**Step 6: Compatibility check (no common ion with precipitation issue)**
- KNO₃ → K⁺ + NO₃⁻
- Urea → CO(NH₂)₂ (molecular, no ions)
- No Ca²⁺ + SO₄²⁻ combination → **Compatible** ✓

**Result:** The mix is physically and chemically compatible.

## 3.2 Derivation: Salt-out Temperature from van't Hoff Equation

**Problem:** A 10-34-0 liquid fertilizer (APP) has $\Delta H_{sol}$ = +15.2 kJ/mol. Find the solubility at 10°C given $S_{25°C} = 480$ g/L.

**Step 1: Convert temperatures to Kelvin**

$$T_1 = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15 \text{ K}$$
$$T_2 = 10 + 273.15 = 283.15 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2: Apply van't Hoff equation**

$$\ln\left(\frac{S_2}{S_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{sol}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 3: Substitute values**

$$\ln\left(\frac{S_2}{480}\right) = -\frac{15200}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{283.15} - \frac{1}{298.15}\right)$$

**Step 4: Calculate temperature term**

$$\frac{1}{283.15} - \frac{1}{298.15} = 0.003532 - 0.003354 = 1.78 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 5: Compute exponent**

$$-\frac{15200}{8.314} \times 1.78 \times 10^{-4} = -1828.5 \times 1.78 \times 10^{-4} = -0.3255$$

**Step 6: Solve for S₂**

$$\frac{S_2}{480} = e^{-0.3255} = 0.722$$

$$S_2 = 480 \times 0.722 = 346.6 \text{ g/L}$$

**Result:** Solubility at 10°C = 346.6 g/L (significantly lower — risk of precipitation in cold weather).

## 3.3 Derivation: Economic Threshold Level (ETL) for Rice Stem Borer

**Problem:** In a rice field, control cost = ₹2000/ha, market value of grain = ₹1800/q, yield loss per larva = 0.6 q/ha, control measure reduces pest by 80%. Calculate EIL and ETL.

**Step 1: Apply Stern's EIL equation**

$$EIL = \frac{C}{V \times D \times K}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$EIL = \frac{2000}{1800 \times 0.6 \times 0.8}$$

**Step 3: Calculate denominator**

$$1800 \times 0.6 \times 0.8 = 864$$

**Step 4: Compute EIL**

$$EIL = \frac{2000}{864} = 2.31 \text{ larvae per m}^2$$

**Step 5: ETL (typically 0.5 × EIL for fast-acting chemical control)**

$$ETL = 0.5 \times 2.31 = 1.16 \approx 1 \text{ larva per m}^2$$

**Result:** Spray when pest density reaches 1 larva/m² (or equivalently 5% dead hearts).

## 3.4 Derivation: Fertilizer Dose Calculation (NPK for Rice)

**Problem:** Recommended N-P-K for a rice crop is 90-45-45 kg/ha. Farmer has access to Urea (46% N), SSP (16% P₂O₅), MOP (60% K₂O). Calculate fertilizer doses.

**Step 1: Urea dose for 90 kg N/ha**

$$D_{urea} = \frac{N_{req}}{\%N/100} = \frac{90}{0.46} = 195.65 \text{ kg/ha}$$

**Step 2: SSP dose for 45 kg P₂O₅/ha**

$$D_{SSP} = \frac{45}{0.16} = 281.25 \text{ kg/ha}$$

**Step 3: MOP dose for 45 kg K₂O/ha**

$$D_{MOP} = \frac{45}{0.60} = 75 \text{ kg/ha}$$

**Step 4: Verify nutrient output**

- N from Urea: $195.65 \times 0.46 = 90$ kg ✓
- P from SSP: $281.25 \times 0.16 = 45$ kg ✓
- K from MOP: $75 \times 0.60 = 45$ kg ✓

**Step 5: Calculate total fertilizer cost** (if Urea=₹12/kg, SSP=₹10/kg, MOP=₹20/kg)

$$Cost = (195.65 \times 12) + (281.25 \times 10) + (75 \times 20)$$
$$Cost = 2347.8 + 2812.5 + 1500 = 6660.3 \text{ ₹/ha}$$

**Result:** Apply 195.65 kg Urea + 281.25 kg SSP + 75 kg MOP per hectare, costing approximately ₹6660.

## 3.5 Derivation: Plant Population and Seed Rate

**Problem:** Rice variety with TGW = 24 g, germination % = 85%, recommended spacing = 15×10 cm, field area = 1 ha. Calculate seed rate.

**Step 1: Calculate plants per m²**

$$PP = \frac{1}{0.15 \times 0.10} = \frac{1}{0.015} = 66.67 \text{ plants/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate plants per hectare**

$$PP_{ha} = 66.67 \times 10000 = 6,66,667 \text{ plants/ha}$$

**Step 3: Calculate seed rate (accounting for germination)**

$$SR = \frac{PP_{ha} \times TGW}{Germ\% \times 1000 \times 10}$$

$$SR = \frac{6,66,667 \times 24}{85 \times 10000}$$

$$SR = \frac{1.6 \times 10^7}{8.5 \times 10^5} = 18.82 \text{ kg/ha}$$

**Step 4: Add 20% buffer for contingencies**

$$SR_{final} = 18.82 \times 1.20 = 22.58 \approx 23 \text{ kg/ha}$$

**Result:** Seed rate ≈ 23 kg/ha for direct-seeded rice under puddle conditions.

## 3.6 Derivation: Water Use Efficiency (WUE) in Drip Irrigation

**Problem:** Banana yield = 50 t/ha, water applied through drip = 8000 m³/ha. Calculate WUE.

**Step 1: Convert yield to kg/ha**

$$Y = 50 \times 1000 = 50,000 \text{ kg/ha}$$

**Step 2: Apply WUE formula**

$$WUE = \frac{Y}{W_{applied}} = \frac{50,000}{8000} = 6.25 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Result:** WUE = 6.25 kg/m³ (compared to flood irrigation WUE of 1.5 kg/m³, drip is 4× more efficient).

## 3.7 Derivation: Soil Organic Carbon (Walkley-Black Method)

**Problem:** Blank titration = 25 mL, sample titration = 18 mL, Normality of FeSO₄ = 0.5 N, Sample weight = 1 g, Factor = 1.33.

**Step 1: Calculate organic carbon %**

$$OC\% = \frac{(B-S) \times N \times 0.003 \times 100 \times f}{W}$$

Where:
- $B$ = Blank = 25 mL
- $S$ = Sample = 18 mL
- $N$ = Normality = 0.5
- $0.003$ = milli-equivalent weight of carbon
- $f$ = correction factor = 1.33
- $W$ = Sample weight = 1 g

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$OC\% = \frac{(25-18) \times 0.5 \times 0.003 \times 100 \times 1.33}{1}$$

$$OC\% = 7 \times 0.5 \times 0.003 \times 100 \times 1.33$$

$$OC\% = 7 \times 0.0015 \times 100 \times 1.33$$

$$OC\% = 1.05 \times 1.33 = 1.397\%$$

**Result:** Soil OC = 1.40% (medium-to-high range, suitable for most crops).

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Compatibility of Common Liquid Fertilizers

| Tank Mix Component 1 | Tank Mix Component 2 | Compatibility | Reason |
|---------------------|---------------------|---------------|--------|
| UAN (Urea Ammonium Nitrate) | Ammonium Polyphosphate (APP) | **Compatible** | No common ion precipitation |
| Ca(NO₃)₂ (Calcium Nitrate) | K₂SO₄ (Potassium Sulphate) | **Incompatible** | CaSO₄ precipitates |
| MgSO₄ | KNO₃ | **Compatible** | Both highly soluble |
| Urea | Ammonium Sulphate | **Compatible** | No common problematic ion |
| Ammonium Nitrate | Potassium Chloride | **Compatible** | No precipitation |
| FeSO₄ (Iron Sulphate) | Lime (Ca hydroxide) | **Incompatible** | Fe(OH)₃ precipitates |
| ZnSO₄ | Phosphate (H₃PO₄) | **Incompatible** | Zn₃(PO₄)₂ precipitates |
| UAN | Magnesium Nitrate | **Compatible** | All nitrates soluble |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Define liquid fertilizer. List any two advantages of liquid fertilizers over solid fertilizers.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A liquid fertilizer is a nutrient formulation in which the essential plant nutrients are either fully dissolved (clear solution) or suspended in a liquid carrier (typically water) and applied to crops through soil, foliar, or fertigation methods.

> **Advantages (2 marks — any two):**
> 1. **Easy and uniform application** through drip/sprinkler systems (fertigation)
> 2. **Rapid plant uptake** since nutrients are already in solution
> 3. **Uniform distribution** of nutrients in the field
> 4. **Lower labour cost** for handling and application
> 5. **Compatibility** with pesticides in tank mix
> 6. **Accurate dosing** possible

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2023]
**Q: What is the Disease Triangle? Explain briefly.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Disease Triangle is a fundamental concept in plant pathology stating that a plant disease can occur only when **three factors are simultaneously present** (1 mark):

1. **Susceptible Host** (1 mark) — A plant variety that lacks resistance to the pathogen
2. **Virulent Pathogen** (1 mark) — A disease-causing organism with sufficient inoculum
3. **Favourable Environment** (1 mark) — Suitable temperature, humidity, leaf wetness for pathogen growth

Removing any one factor prevents disease development — this is the basis of IPM.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A-1 (Option A) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the solubility and compatibility characteristics of liquid fertilizers used in agriculture. Discuss the factors affecting solubility with relevant equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List out the major pests and diseases of rice in Kerala. Describe the integrated management of rice stem borer (*Scirpophaga incertulas*). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution (a) — Solubility and Compatibility of Liquid Fertilizers (7 marks)

**1. Definition and Types (2 marks):**
Liquid fertilizers are nutrient solutions in which N, P, K, and micronutrients are dissolved in water. Types include clear liquid fertilizers (UAN 32-0-0, APP 10-34-0), suspension fertilizers, and foliar nutrient solutions.

**2. Solubility and Factors (3 marks):**

$$S = \frac{m_{solute}}{m_{solvent}} \times 100 \quad \text{(g/100g water)}$$

**Factors affecting solubility:**
- **Temperature:** Most salts show positive temperature dependence (endothermic dissolution)
- **pH of solution:** Affects ionization and salt stability
- **Common ion effect:** Presence of a common ion reduces solubility (Le Chatelier's principle)
- **Salt-out temperature:** Critical for storage in cold climates
- **Ionic strength of solution:** Higher ionic strength reduces solubility of added salts

**3. Compatibility (2 marks):**
- **Chemical compatibility:** No precipitation reactions (e.g., Ca(NO₃)₂ + K₂SO₄ → CaSO₄ ↓)
- **Physical compatibility:** No phase separation, viscosity changes
- **Rule:** Use **jar test** before tank mixing
- **Precipitation check** via solubility product: $K_{sp} = [Ca^{2+}][SO_4^{2-}]$; precipitation occurs when ion product > Ksp

#### Solution (b) — Pests & Diseases of Rice and Management of Stem Borer (7 marks)

**Major Pests of Rice in Kerala (2 marks):**

| Pest | Scientific Name | Damage |
|------|----------------|--------|
| Rice stem borer | *Scirpophaga incertulas* | Dead heart, white ear |
| Brown plant hopper | *Nilaparvata lugens* | Hopper burn |
| Green leafhopper | *Nephotettix virescens* | Tungro virus vector |
| Rice leaf folder | *Cnaphalocrocis medinalis* | Folded leaves |
| Gall midge | *Orseolia oryzae* | Silver shoot |

**Major Diseases of Rice in Kerala (2 marks):**

| Disease | Pathogen | Symptom |
|---------|----------|---------|
| Rice blast | *Magnaporthe oryzae* (fungus) | Diamond-shaped leaf spots |
| Bacterial leaf blight | *Xanthomonas oryzae* | Yellow lesions from tip |
| Sheath blight | *Rhizoctonia solani* | Oval grey lesions on sheath |
| False smut | *Ustilaginoidea virens* | Yellow balls in panicle |
| Tungro | Rice tungro virus | Yellow-orange discolouration |

**Integrated Management of Rice Stem Borer (3 marks):**

1. **Cultural control (1 mark):**
   - Destroy stubble and infested tillers
   - Synchronized planting across the village
   - Use resistant varieties (e.g., IR-64, Jyothi)
   - Clip seedling tips before transplanting

2. **Mechanical control (0.5 mark):**
   - Light traps @ 1/ha to monitor and kill adult moths
   - Pheromone traps (@5/ha) with *Scirpophaga* lures

3. **Biological control (0.5 mark):**
   - Release *Trichogramma japonicum* @ 50,000/ha/week for 4 weeks during tillering
   - Spray *Bacillus thuringiensis* (Bt) formulations

4. **Chemical control (1 mark) — last resort:**
   - Apply Cartap hydrochloride 50 SP @ 1 kg/ha OR
   - Chlorantraniliprole 18.5 SC @ 0.3 mL/L OR
   - Fipronil 5 SC @ 2 mL/L

---

### Question B-1 (Option B) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Q: (a)** Describe the principles and components of Integrated Nutrient Management (INM) and Integrated Pest Management (IPM) in field crops. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the package of practices for cultivation of coconut in Kerala. Discuss the major pests and diseases of coconut and their management. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution (a) — Principles of INM and IPM (7 Marks)

**1. INM — Definition and Principles (2 marks):**
INM is the combined use of organic manures, green manures, biofertilizers, crop residues, and chemical fertilizers to maintain soil fertility and ensure sustainable crop production.

**Principles:**
- Maintain soil organic matter > 0.8% SOC
- Balance nutrient supply (NPK + S + Zn)
- Match nutrient supply to crop demand at critical stages
- Reduce chemical fertilizer use through biological N-fixation

**Components (2 marks):**

| Component | Typical N Contribution | Example |
|-----------|------------------------|---------|
| Chemical fertilizer | 50% | Urea, DAP, MOP |
| FYM/Compost | 20-25% | 10-12 t/ha FYM |
| Green manure | 15-20% | Sesbania, Sunnhemp (in-situ) |
| Biofertilizer | 10% | Azolla, Rhizobium, PSB, Azospirillum |

**2. IPM — Definition and Principles (2 marks):**
IPM is a sustainable pest control strategy that uses compatible methods to keep pest populations below ETL, minimizing environmental and economic risks.

**Principles:**
- **Prevention** is the first line of defence
- Use **multiple control methods** in a compatible manner
- Apply **monitoring** (scouting, traps) before deciding control
- Use **chemical control** as a last resort
- Conserve natural enemies

**Components (1 mark):**
1. **Cultural:** Crop rotation, resistant varieties, sanitation, sowing time
2. **Mechanical:** Light traps, sticky traps, hand picking, pheromone traps
3. **Biological:** Parasitoids (*Trichogramma*), predators (*Crysoperla*), pathogens (NPV, *Beauveria*)
4. **Botanical:** Neem oil, Pongamia, NSKE 5%
5. **Chemical:** Selective insecticides, rotation of MoA groups

#### Solution (b) — Coconut Cultivation Package and Pest/Disease Management (7 Marks)

**1. Package of Practices (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Recommendation |
|-----------|----------------|
| Variety | West Coast Tall (WCT), Chowghat Orange Dwarf (COD), T x D hybrids |
| Soil | Laterite, alluvial, well-drained, pH 5.5-7.5 |
| Spacing | 7.5 × 7.5 m (square) or 8 × 8 m |
| Pit size | 1.2 × 1.2 × 1.2 m, filled with topsoil + 50 kg FYM + 1 kg neem cake |
| Planting time | May-June (with onset of monsoon) |
| Fertilizer | 0.5 kg N + 0.32 kg P₂O₅ + 1.2 kg K₂O per palm per year (split into 2 doses) |
| Irrigation | 45 L/palm/day in dry months; drip @ 32 L/day recommended |
| Intercropping | Banana, pepper (on trunk), cocoa, nutmeg, groundnut, tapioca |
| Bearing age | 6-8 years (WCT), 4-5 years (hybrids) |
| Yield | 60-80 nuts/palm/year (WCT), 100-150 nuts (hybrids) |

**2. Major Pests of Coconut (2 marks):**

| Pest | Damage | Management |
|------|--------|------------|
| Rhinoceros beetle (*Oryctes rhinoceros*) | V-shaped cuts on fronds, bore into crown | Crown cleaning, pheromone traps, Metarhizium anisopliae |
| Red palm weevil (*Rhynchophorus ferrugineus*) | Internal damage, frond wilting | Pheromone traps, prophylactic stem injection, *Steinernema* nematode |
| Black-headed caterpillar (*Opisina arenosella*) | Defoliation | Cut & burn infested leaves, release *Trichogramma*, *Goniozus nephantidis* |
| Eriophyid mite | Triangular brown patches on nuts | Neem oil spray, sulphur dust, Hexythiazox |

**3. Major Diseases of Coconut (2 marks):**

| Disease | Causal Organism | Management |
|---------|----------------|------------|
| Bud rot | *Phytophthora palmivora* | Bordeaux paste on bud, spray 1% Bordeaux mixture, remove infected tissue |
| Basal stem rot (Ganoderma wilt) | *Ganoderma lucidum* | Soil drenching with Hexaconazole, avoid water stagnation, neem cake application |
| Root wilt | Phytoplasma | Tolerant varieties (WCT), integrated nutrient management, mulching |
| Leaf blight | *Bipolaris incurvata* | Spray Mancozeb 0.3% |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Misdistributing marks:** Students often write 4 pages for 7-mark questions and 2 pages for 7-mark questions in the same 14-mark slot. The 14-mark answer should be split as **7 + 7 marks**. Spend equal time on (a) and (b).
> 2. **Missing sub-parts:** Always read "describe" (full explanation) vs "list" (just enumeration) vs "state" (concise point).
> 3. **Not drawing diagrams:** Even rough block diagrams of disease cycle, IPM hierarchy, or crop geometry earn easy marks.
> 4. **Skipping Kerala context:** KTU loves Kerala-specific examples — Pokkali rice, coconut, tapioca, pepper, banana. Always use Kerala data, not Punjab data.
> 5. **Forgetting units:** Always write q/ha, kg/ha, ₹/ha with units. Marks are deducted for unitless numerical answers.
> 6. **Mixing up INM and IPM:** INM = nutrients only; IPM = pests only; IWM = water only. Do not confuse.

---

## 5.3 Additional Practice Questions

### Question 3 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Q: (a)** Discuss the package of practices for cultivation of rice (paddy) under Kerala conditions. Include land preparation, varieties, sowing, water, and nutrient management. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of organic farming. Describe the certification process and key practices of organic farming in India. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — Rice Cultivation in Kerala (7 Marks)

**1. Land Preparation (1.5 marks):**
- Plough with mould board plough or power tiller (2-3 times)
- Pudding in standing water (5-8 cm) for transplanting
- Level the field with wooden plank (levelling)
- Apply lime @ 600 kg/ha in acidic laterite soils 2-3 weeks before puddling
- Apply FYM @ 10 t/ha before last puddling

**2. Varieties (1.5 marks):**

| Variety | Duration | Special Feature |
|---------|----------|-----------------|
| Jyothi | 120-125 days | High-yielding, Kerala popular |
| IR-64 | 117-120 days | Wide adaptability |
| Uma | 105-110 days | Short duration, drought-escape |
| Aiswarya | 120 days | Blast resistant |
| Pokkali | 110-120 days | Salt-tolerant organic rice |

**3. Sowing/Transplanting (1.5 marks):**
- Nursery: 1 kg seed per 100 m² for 1 ha transplanting
- Seed rate: 60-75 kg/ha for transplanting
- Seed treatment: Carbendazim @ 2 g/kg + Azospirillum @ 200 g/kg
- Transplanting at 25-30 days old seedlings, 2-3 seedlings/hill
- Spacing: 15 × 10 cm (or 20 × 10 cm for hybrids)
- Line planting for weed management and uniform growth

**4. Nutrient Management (1.5 marks):**
- NPK: 90-45-45 kg/ha for HYVs; 120-60-60 for hybrids
- N in 3 splits: 50% basal + 25% tillering + 25% panicle initiation
- Apply Azospirillum @ 2 kg/ha + Azolla dual inoculation
- Green manure: Sesbania/Sunn hemp 50 days before puddling
- Apply K with N at active tillering

**5. Water Management (1 mark):**
- Continuous submergence (2-5 cm) during tillering to PI
- 5-7 cm during grain filling
- Drain 1 week before harvest
- Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD) saves 25-30% water
- SRI method: 2 cm water, alternate wetting/drying

#### Model Solution (b) — Organic Farming (7 Marks)

**1. Definition and Principles (2 marks):**
Organic farming is a production system that **prohibits synthetic agrochemicals** and instead relies on **crop rotations, organic manures, biofertilizers, biopesticides, and biological control** for sustainable production.

**Key Principles (1 mark):**
- **Health:** Sustain soil, plant, animal, human, and planet health
- **Ecology:** Based on living ecological systems and cycles
- **Fairness:** Equity, respect, and justice for all participants
- **Care:** Manage agricultural systems responsibly

**2. Key Practices (2 marks):**

| Practice | Description | Example |
|----------|-------------|---------|
| Crop rotation | Alternating crops to break pest/disease cycle | Rice-Pulse-Sesbania |
| Green manuring | Growing legumes in-situ and ploughing in | Sesbania, Sunnhemp |
| Composting | Decomposition of organic matter | FYM, Vermicompost, NADEP |
| Biofertilizers | Microbial inoculants | Rhizobium, PSB, Azolla |
| Biopesticides | Natural enemies and botanicals | NPV, Bt, Neem, Trichoderma |
| Mulching | Cover to conserve moisture and suppress weeds | Straw, plastic, live mulch |

**3. Certification Process in India (2 marks):**
- **NPOP** (National Programme for Organic Production) by APEDA, GoI
- **Accreditation:** By APEDA-accredited certification bodies
- **Standards:** No synthetic chemicals for 2-3 years (conversion period)
- **Buffer zone:** 5-10 m between organic and conventional
- **PGS-India** (Participatory Guarantee System): For small/marginal farmers, group-based, low-cost
- **Records:** Detailed farm diary, input-output registers, traceability
- **Logo:** "India Organic" logo for certified products

**4. Components of Organic Farming (1 mark — additional point):**
- Organic nutrient management: FYM, vermicompost, neem cake, bone meal, rock phosphate
- Plant protection: Neem oil 0.5%, *Trichoderma viride* 0.2%, NPV 250 LE/ha
- Weed management: Mulching, hand weeding, stale seed bed technique

### Question 4 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Q: (a)** Define ETL and EIL. A farmer in Palakkad has the following data for rice stem borer management. Calculate EIL and ETL.
- Cost of insecticidal control: ₹3000/ha
- Market value of paddy: ₹2200/q
- Yield loss per larva: 0.5 q/ha
- Control efficacy: 75%
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write short notes on: (i) Sustainable agriculture, (ii) Natural farming, (iii) Organic farming. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — ETL/EIL Calculation (7 Marks)

**1. Definitions (2 marks):**
- **EIL (Economic Injury Level):** The lowest pest population density that will cause economic damage equal to the cost of control.
- **ETL (Economic Threshold Level):** The pest density at which control measures should be applied to prevent the population from reaching EIL (typically 0.5-0.75 × EIL).

**2. EIL Calculation using Stern's Equation (3 marks):**

$$EIL = \frac{C}{V \times D \times K}$$

Where:
- $C$ = ₹3000/ha
- $V$ = ₹2200/q
- $D$ = 0.5 q/ha/larva
- $K$ = 0.75 (75% control)

$$EIL = \frac{3000}{2200 \times 0.5 \times 0.75}$$

$$EIL = \frac{3000}{825} = 3.64 \text{ larvae/m}^2$$

**3. ETL Calculation (2 marks):**

$$ETL = 0.5 \times EIL = 0.5 \times 3.64 = 1.82 \text{ larvae/m}^2 \approx 2 \text{ larvae/m}^2$$

**Decision:** When scouting reveals ≥ 2 larvae/m² (or 5% dead hearts), the farmer should apply insecticidal control.

#### Model Solution (b) — Short Notes on Sustainable, Natural, and Organic Farming (7 Marks)

**(i) Sustainable Agriculture (2.5 marks):**
- A holistic production system meeting present needs without compromising future generation capacity
- Three pillars: **Environmental, Economic, Social**
- Practices: INM, IPM, IWM, conservation agriculture, agroforestry
- Indicators: SOC > 0.8%, WUE > 1.5 kg/m³, BCR > 2
- Examples: Zero-till wheat, System of Rice Intensification (SRI), Integrated Farming Systems (IFS)

**(ii) Natural Farming (2 marks):**
- Also called **Zero Budget Natural Farming (ZBNF)** developed by Subhash Palekar
- **Four pillars:** Bijamrita (seed treatment), Jeevamrita (microbial culture), Mulch (achchadana), Whapasa (moisture + air)
- **Zero external input** — relies on on-farm resources
- Cow-based farming: 1 cow per 30 acres
- Lower cost of production, suitable for marginal farmers

**(iii) Organic Farming (2.5 marks):**
- Production system **excluding synthetic agrochemicals**
- **Permitted inputs:** Organic manures, biofertilizers, biopesticides, botanical pesticides
- **Standards:** NPOP, PGS-India, IFOAM
- **Conversion period:** 2-3 years
- **Premium price:** 20-30% higher in market
- Examples: Pokkali rice, virgin coconut oil, Sikkim (100% organic state)

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4**

### Liquid Fertilizers
- [ ] Liquid fertilizers: UAN (32-0-0), APP (10-34-0) — major types
- [ ] Salt Index concept: SI of NaNO₃ = 100; Urea = 75.4
- [ ] **Common ion effect** → Ca²⁺ + SO₄²⁻ → CaSO₄ ↓ (avoid mixing)
- [ ] Jar test before tank mixing is **mandatory**
- [ ] pH range of safe foliar spray: **5.5 – 7.0**
- [ ] Solubility equation: $S = m_{solute}/m_{solvent} \times 100$
- [ ] van't Hoff equation governs temperature-solubility relation

### Pest & Disease Management
- [ ] **Disease Triangle:** Host + Pathogen + Environment → Disease
- [ ] EIL = C/(V·D·K); ETL = 0.5 × EIL (typical)
- [ ] IPM hierarchy: Cultural → Mechanical → Biological → Botanical → Chemical
- [ ] Key biocontrol agents: *Trichogramma* (egg parasitoid), NPV, *Beauveria*
- [ ] **Rice major pests:** Stem borer, BPH, leaf folder, gall midge, Gandhi bug
- [ ] **Rice major diseases:** Blast, BLB, sheath blight, tungro, false smut

### Field Crops — Kerala
- [ ] **Rice:** 90-45-45 kg NPK/ha, spacing 15×10 cm, seed rate ~60 kg/ha (transplanted)
- [ ] **Coconut:** Spacing 7.5×7.5 m, fertilizer 0.5-1.2 kg NPK per palm
- [ ] **Banana:** Spacing 2×2 m, drip irrigation @ 100% ETc
- [ ] **Pepper:** Trained on coconut/trees (mixed cropping)
- [ ] **Tapioca:** Spacing 90×90 cm, 2000 stems/ha
- [ ] Sugarcane: Kerala's midland crop, spacing 75-90 cm rows
- [ ] Groundnut: Spacing 30×10 cm, seed rate 100-120 kg/ha
- [ ] Sesamum: Small-seeded oil crop, Kerala minor

### Horticultural Crops
- [ ] **Vegetables:** Tomato, brinjal, chilli, cucurbits (snake gourd, bitter gourd, cucumber)
- [ ] **Fruits:** Coconut, banana, mango, jackfruit, cashew, papaya, pineapple
- [ ] **Flowers:** Jasmine, marigold, rose, tuberose, crossandra, anthurium
- [ ] Banana propagation: **Suckers** (corms); 30,000-35,000 plants/ha
- [ ] Pineapple: Plant density 50,000-60,000/ha, drip irrigation
- [ ] Anthurium needs **polyhouse** (70% shade, 80% humidity)

### Sustainable & Organic Agriculture
- [ ] **ZBNF (Palekar):** Bijamrita, Jeevamrita, Mulch, Whapasa
- [ ] **NPOP** = National Programme for Organic Production
- [ ] **PGS-India** = Participatory Guarantee System (small farmers)
- [ ] Conversion period: **2-3 years** for organic certification
- [ ] Buffer zone: **5-10 m** between organic & conventional
- [ ] **IFS (Integrated Farming System):** Crop + Livestock + Fishery + Agroforestry
- [ ] SRI (System of Rice Intensification): Single seedling, wider spacing, AWD water management
- [ ] SOC target: **> 0.8%** in tropical soils
- [ ] SOC = **soil organic carbon** (Walkley-Black method)
